items.subtopic: Inflammatory Bowel Disease | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)
items.title: Rising Incidence of IBD in Developing Countries
items.content: The incidence of Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD), including Ulcerative Colitis (UC) and Crohn's Disease (CD), is increasing in newly industrialized countries, particularly those experiencing urbanization and Westernization. This trend mirrors the historical rise seen in Western countries.
keyPoints: Prevalence of IBD >0.3% in North America, Oceania, and Europe.
Annual percent change: +11.1% (95% CI, 4.8–17.8%) for CD and +14.9% (95% CI, 10.4–19.6%) for UC in Brazil; +4.0% (95% CI, 1.0–7.1%) for CD and +4.8% (95% CI, 1.8–8.0%) for UC in Taiwan.
Mean annual IBD incidence per 100,000: 1.50 (95% CI, 1.43–1.57) across Asia Pacific; highest incidences in India (9.31; 95% CI, 8.38–10.31) and China (3.64; 95% CI, 2.97–4.42).
Environmental factors such as diet changes, sunlight exposure, socioeconomic status, and hygiene are likely contributors.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · Inflammatory Bowel Disease | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)

items.subtopic: Inflammatory Bowel Disease | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)
items.title: Demographics of IBD Incidence
items.content: IBD primarily affects individuals in their second to fourth decades, with a notable peak incidence among those aged 20–29 years. There is also a modest rise in incidence between the seventh and ninth decades.
keyPoints: Peak incidence: second to fourth decades.
78% of CD studies and 51% of UC studies report highest incidence in ages 20–29.
Second rise in incidence occurs between seventh and ninth decades.
Female-to-male ratio ranges from 0.51 to 1.58 for UC and 0.34 to 1.65 for CD, indicating non-gender-specific diagnosis.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · Inflammatory Bowel Disease | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)

items.subtopic: Inflammatory Bowel Disease | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)
items.title: Environmental Risk Factors in IBD
items.content: Several environmental factors are associated with an increased risk of developing IBD, including smoking, diet, and infections.
keyPoints: Smoking has opposite effects on UC (OR 0.58) and CD (OR 1.76).
Previous appendectomy with confirmed appendicitis reduces UC risk by 13–26%.
Oral contraceptive use increases CD risk, especially among current users (hazard ratio up to 2.82).
Breast-feeding may protect against IBD development.
Infections in the first year of life increase IBD risk, particularly with pathogens like Salmonella and Shigella.
Diet high in animal protein, sugars, sweets, oils, fish, shellfish, dietary fat (especially omega-6 fatty acids), and low in omega-3 fatty acids increases IBD risk.
Vitamin D may have a protective effect against CD.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · Inflammatory Bowel Disease | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)

items.subtopic: GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 2) | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: IBD PHENOTYPES | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Familial Risk and Twin Concordance in IBD
items.content: IBD has a significant familial component, with 5–10% of patients having a family history. First-degree relatives have the strongest risk factor for developing IBD. Children of parents with UC or CD face increased risks (fourfold for UC, eightfold for CD). Twin studies show high concordance rates in monozygotic twins (38–58% for CD and 6–18% for UC) compared to dizygotic twins (4% for CD, 0–2% for UC), indicating a genetic component.
keyPoints: 5–10% of IBD patients have familial disease
First-degree relatives significantly increase risk
Children of parents with UC: ~fourfold increased risk
Children of parents with CD: ~eightfold increased risk
Monozygotic twin concordance for CD: 38–58%
Monozygotic twin concordance for UC: 6–18%
Dizygotic twin concordance for CD: 4%
Dizygotic twin concordance for UC: 0–2%
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 2) | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: IBD PHENOTYPES | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: IBD PHENOTYPES | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Racial Differences in IBD Phenotypes and Extraintestinal Manifestations
items.content: IBD phenotypes vary by race, reflecting genetic and environmental influences. Blacks and Latinxs often have ileocolonic CD, while East Asians show high rates of perianal disease (30.3–58.8%). UC is more likely to be pancolonic among Black, Latinx, and Asian patients. Older Asian UC patients (>60) experience aggressive disease courses. Extraintestinal manifestations differ: joint involvement in Blacks (15.7–29.6%) and ocular issues in African Americans (7.1–13%), with dermatologic symptoms common in Latinxes (10–13%).
keyPoints: Blacks/Latinxs: ileocolonic CD distribution
East Asians: perianal disease 30.3–58.8%
UC more pancolonic among Black, Latinx, Asian patients
Older Asian UC patients (>60): aggressive course
Joint involvement in Blacks: 15.7–29.6%
Ocular issues in African Americans: 7.1–13%
Dermatologic symptoms in Latinxes: 10–13%
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 2) | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: IBD PHENOTYPES | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Pathogenesis of IBD as a Dysregulated Immune Response
items.content: IBD results from disrupted homeostasis among the microbiota, intestinal epithelial cells (IECs), and immune cells. Environmental factors like smoking and antibiotics, alongside genetic predispositions, can lead to chronic inflammation. The disease is considered an inappropriate immune response to commensal microbiota, potentially with autoimmunity components. Normally, regulatory pathways in the gut prevent full immunologic responses to commensals; however, these are dysregulated in IBD.
keyPoints: IBD involves disrupted homeostasis among microbiota, IECs, immune cells
Environmental factors: smoking, antibiotics, enteropathogens
Genetic predispositions contribute to susceptibility
IBD as an inappropriate immune response to commensal microbiota
Potential autoimmunity component
Regulatory pathways in gut prevent full immunologic responses
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 2) | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: IBD PHENOTYPES | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Genetic Underpinnings in IBD: Monogenic vs. Polygenic Origins
items.content: IBD can have a monogenic etiology, particularly in severe cases like VEOIBD and infantile IBD, where over 60 gene defects have been identified through whole-exome sequencing (WES). However, most pediatric and adult IBD cases are polygenic. This distinction is crucial for understanding disease mechanisms and potential therapeutic targets.
keyPoints: More than 60 different gene defects identified in VEOIBD and infantile IBD by WES.
Monogenic mutations include genes like IL-10, IL-10R, CTLA4, NCF2, XIAP, LRBA, TTC7.
Polygenic nature elucidated through GWAS identifying ~240 genetic loci.
Only ~20% of disease risk explained by current genetic information.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS

items.title: Shared Genetic Risk Factors and Therapeutic Implications in IBD
items.content: Genetic studies reveal shared risk factors between IBD and other immune-mediated diseases, suggesting common immunogenetic pathways. This overlap influences therapeutic responses, particularly to biologic therapies like anti-TNF treatments.
keyPoints: Shared genetic risk factors with rheumatoid arthritis (TNFAIP3), psoriasis (IL23R, IL12B), ankylosing spondylitis (IL23R), type 1 diabetes mellitus (IL10, PTPN2), asthma (ORMDL3), systemic lupus erythematosus (TNFAIP3, IL10).
Common responsiveness to biologic therapies such as anti-TNF.
Genetic similarities account for overlapping immunopathogenesis in IBD subtypes.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS

items.title: Functional Impact of Genetic Variants on IBD Pathogenesis
items.content: Genetic variants associated with IBD affect fundamental cellular processes, innate and adaptive immunity, and inflammation resolution. These genetic susceptibilities incrementally contribute to disease risk and influence the activities of immune and nonimmune cells in the intestines.
keyPoints: Genes involved in unfolded protein response, autophagy, metabolism, innate immunity, adaptive immunity, and inflammation resolution.
NOD2 polymorphisms linked with fibrostenosing CD; ATG16L1 associated with fistulizing disease, especially in the ileum.
Clinical utility of genetic risk factors for diagnosis, prognosis, and therapeutic responses remains undefined.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS

items.title: Role of Commensal Microbiota in IBD Pathogenesis
items.content: The commensal microbiota is crucial in the pathogenesis of Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD). Humans acquire their gut microbiota from maternal and environmental sources, achieving a stable configuration by age 3. This unique microbial composition influences host epithelial and immune functions through structural components and metabolic activity, potentially affecting IBD risk later in life.
keyPoints: Humans are born sterile and acquire microbiota initially from the mother during birth.
A stable gut microbiota with up to 1000 species is achieved by age 3, reaching a biomass of ~10^12 colony-forming units per gram of feces.
The microbiota composition is influenced by host factors (immune, epithelial), environmental factors (diet, antibiotics), and genetic factors (e.g., NOD2).
Dysbiosis in IBD patients includes increased Proteobacteria (e.g., enteroinvasive E. coli) and decreased Firmicutes (e.g., Faecalibacterium prausnitzii).
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · COMMENSAL MICROBIOTA AND IBD

items.title: Impact of Microbiota Alteration on CD Management
items.content: Altering the intestinal microbiota can improve Crohn's Disease (CD) symptoms. Treatments such as metronidazole, ciprofloxacin, and elemental diets modify the microbiota composition, potentially reducing disease activity. Additionally, fecal diversion demonstrates that luminal contents exacerbate CD, highlighting the role of microbiota in disease management.
keyPoints: Agents like metronidazole, ciprofloxacin, and elemental diets can improve CD by altering the intestinal microbiota.
Fecal diversion may reduce CD symptoms, indicating the exacerbating role of luminal contents.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · COMMENSAL MICROBIOTA AND IBD

items.title: Mechanisms of Oral Tolerance and its Failure in IBD
items.content: Oral tolerance is a process where the mucosal immune system avoids inflammatory responses to luminal contents, involving mechanisms like deletion or anergy of antigen-reactive T cells, and induction of anti-inflammatory CD4+ T regulatory cells. In IBD, this regulation fails, leading to uncontrolled inflammation.
keyPoints: Oral tolerance involves the suppression of immune response via mechanisms such as deletion/anergy of T cells and induction of T regulatory cells expressing FoxP3.
Anti-inflammatory cytokines involved include IL-10, IL-35, and TGF-$\beta$.
In IBD, these suppressive mechanisms are altered, resulting in uncontrolled inflammation.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · DEFECTIVE IMMUNE REGULATION IN IBD

items.title: Role of Gene Knockout Models in Understanding IBD Pathogenesis
items.content: Gene knockout and transgenic mouse models have shown that altering specific cytokines, T-cell antigen recognition molecules, or intestinal epithelial cell (IEC) barrier functions can lead to spontaneous colitis or enteritis. These findings suggest a complex interaction between host genetics and the commensal microbiota in IBD pathogenesis.
keyPoints: Gene knockout models targeting IL-2, IL-10, TGF-$\beta$, T-cell antigen receptors, mucus glycoproteins, or NF-$\kappa$B can induce colitis/enteritis.
Most intestinal inflammation in these models requires the presence of commensal microbiota.
Some immune activations are exacerbated by absence of bacteria, highlighting protective roles of microbiota.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · DEFECTIVE IMMUNE REGULATION IN IBD

items.title: Mechanism of Inflammation in IBD: Role of Cytokines and Immune Cells
items.content: Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD) involves a complex inflammatory cascade initiated by abnormal immune sensing. This leads to T-cell and B-cell activation, perpetuating inflammation due to inadequate regulatory pathways. Key cytokines like IL-1, IL-6, IL-12, IL-23, and TNF play crucial roles in promoting tissue damage and fibrogenesis.
keyPoints: Inflammation in IBD is driven by genetic predisposition and environmental factors.
Cytokines such as IL-1, IL-6, IL-12, IL-23, and TNF are central to the inflammatory response.
These cytokines promote fibrogenesis, collagen production, and activation of tissue metalloproteinases.
In IBD, proinflammatory mediators are not adequately regulated by anti-inflammatory cytokines like IL-10 and TGF-β.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · THE INFLAMMATORY CASCADE IN IBD

items.title: T-cell Subsets in IBD: Types and Effects
items.content: Different CD4+ T helper (TH) cell subsets contribute to the inflammatory process in IBD. TH1 cells induce granulomatous inflammation, TH2 cells cause superficial mucosal inflammation, TH9 cells promote allergic-like responses, and TH17 cells are involved in neutrophilic recruitment.
keyPoints: TH1 cells secrete IL-2 and IFN-γ, leading to transmural granulomatous inflammation.
TH2 cells produce IL-4, IL-5, and IL-13, causing superficial mucosal inflammation.
TH9 cells are associated with allergic-like inflammation through IL-9 secretion.
TH17 cells secrete IL-17, IL-21, and IL-22, contributing to neutrophilic recruitment.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · THE INFLAMMATORY CASCADE IN IBD

items.title: Therapeutic Targets in the Inflammatory Cascade of IBD
items.content: Targeting specific components of the inflammatory cascade offers therapeutic benefits in IBD. This includes blocking proinflammatory cytokines or their receptors, inhibiting lymphocyte emigration, and impeding leukocyte recruitment into the intestines.
keyPoints: Antibodies against TNF, IL-12, IL-23, and JAK inhibitors block proinflammatory signaling.
Ozanimod antagonizes S1PR-induced lymphocyte emigration from lymph nodes.
Anti-$\alpha4\beta7$ antibodies impede leukocyte recruitment into the intestines.
Cytokines like IL-10 can inhibit inflammation and promote regulatory T cells.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · THE INFLAMMATORY CASCADE IN IBD

items.title: Histologic Features of Chronic Ulcerative Colitis
items.content: In ulcerative colitis, chronicity is indicated by specific histologic features that help distinguish it from other forms of colitis. Key microscopic changes include distorted crypt architecture and the presence of basal plasma cells with lymphoid aggregates.
keyPoints: Crypt architecture distortion: Crypts may be bifid and reduced in number, often with a gap between crypt bases and muscularis mucosae.
Basal plasma cells and lymphoid aggregates are present in some patients.
Inflammation includes mucosal vascular congestion, edema, focal hemorrhage, and an infiltrate of neutrophils, lymphocytes, plasma cells, and macrophages.
Neutrophil invasion into the epithelium leads to cryptitis and crypt abscesses.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · PATHOLOGY | ULCERATIVE COLITIS: MICROSCOPIC FEATURES

items.title: Ileal Changes in Backwash Ileitis Associated with Ulcerative Colitis
items.content: In patients with ulcerative colitis, backwash ileitis can cause specific histologic changes in the terminal ileum. These include villous atrophy and crypt regeneration, along with increased inflammation.
keyPoints: Villous atrophy is observed in the ileum.
Crypt regeneration occurs alongside increased inflammation.
Increased neutrophil and mononuclear cell infiltration in the lamina propria.
Patchy cryptitis and crypt abscesses are present.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · PATHOLOGY | ULCERATIVE COLITIS: MICROSCOPIC FEATURES

items.subtopic: CROHN'S DISEASE: MACROSCOPIC FEATURES | CROHN'S DISEASE: MICROSCOPIC FEATURES
items.title: Understanding the Distribution and Endoscopic Features of Crohn's Disease
items.content: Crohn's disease (CD) can affect any part of the gastrointestinal tract, with specific patterns in distribution. The terminal ileum is involved in 90% of patients with small-intestinal disease. CD often presents segmentally with skip areas and may involve perianal regions in one-third of cases. Endoscopically, mild disease shows aphthous or superficial ulcerations, while active disease displays a 'cobblestone' appearance due to stellate ulcerations forming islands of mucosa.
keyPoints: CD can affect any part of the GI tract from mouth to anus.
30–40% have small-bowel disease alone; 40–55% involve both small and large intestines; 15–25% have colitis alone.
Terminal ileum is involved in 90% of patients with small-intestinal disease.
CD often segmental with skip areas, unlike UC which almost always involves the rectum.
Perianal disease present in one-third of CD patients, especially those with colonic involvement.
Endoscopic mild CD shows aphthous or superficial ulcerations; active CD shows 'cobblestone' appearance.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE: MACROSCOPIC FEATURES | CROHN'S DISEASE: MICROSCOPIC FEATURES

items.subtopic: CROHN'S DISEASE: MACROSCOPIC FEATURES | CROHN'S DISEASE: MICROSCOPIC FEATURES
items.title: Microscopic Features and Pathological Progression of Crohn's Disease
items.content: The microscopic features of CD include early lesions such as aphthoid ulcerations, focal crypt abscesses with macrophages forming noncaseating granulomas. Granulomas are characteristic but more commonly found in surgical specimens than mucosal biopsies. Other histologic findings include submucosal or subserosal lymphoid aggregates and transmural inflammation leading to deep fissures, fistulous tracts, or abscesses.
keyPoints: Earliest lesions: aphthoid ulcerations, focal crypt abscesses with macrophages forming noncaseating granulomas.
Granulomas are characteristic of CD but more common in surgical specimens than mucosal biopsies.
Histologic features include submucosal or subserosal lymphoid aggregates and transmural inflammation.
Transmural inflammation leads to deep fissures, fistulous tracts, or abscesses.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE: MACROSCOPIC FEATURES | CROHN'S DISEASE: MICROSCOPIC FEATURES

items.title: Recognizing and Classifying Disease Activity in UC
items.content: The severity of symptoms in ulcerative colitis (UC) correlates with the extent of disease. Symptoms range from rectal bleeding, diarrhea, and tenesmus to abdominal pain. The classification of disease activity helps guide treatment decisions.
keyPoints: Symptoms include diarrhea, rectal bleeding, tenesmus, mucus passage, and crampy abdominal pain.
Severity correlates with the extent of disease; proctitis presents with blood-stained mucus and urgency without much abdominal pain.
Disease activity classification is crucial for treatment decisions.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · ULCERATIVE COLITIS

items.title: Diagnostic Tools and Markers in UC
items.content: Diagnosis of UC involves clinical symptoms, laboratory tests, endoscopy, and histology. Key markers include C-reactive protein (CRP), erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), fecal lactoferrin, and calprotectin.
keyPoints: Active disease is associated with elevated CRP, platelet count, ESR, and decreased hemoglobin.
Fecal lactoferrin and calprotectin are sensitive markers for intestinal inflammation.
Diagnosis relies on clinical symptoms, endoscopic appearance, and histology.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · ULCERATIVE COLITIS

items.title: Complications of UC: Toxic Megacolon and Perforation
items.content: Toxic megacolon is a severe complication characterized by colon dilation >6 cm, with significant morbidity. Perforation, though rare, has a high mortality rate.
keyPoints: Toxic megacolon occurs rarely, defined as colon diameter >6 cm, and requires urgent colectomy if not resolved conservatively.
Perforation is the most dangerous complication with ~15% mortality in toxic megacolon cases.
Strictures occur in ~5% of patients and may indicate underlying neoplasia.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · ULCERATIVE COLITIS

items.title: Patterns of Disease and Clinical Manifestations in Ileocolitis
items.content: Crohn's disease can evolve into two main patterns: fibrostenotic obstructing or penetrating fistulous. The site of inflammation, particularly the terminal ileum, influences clinical presentations such as right lower quadrant pain and diarrhea.
keyPoints: Two disease patterns: fibrostenotic obstructing or penetrating fistulous
Common presentation includes chronic episodes of right lower quadrant pain and diarrhea
Weight loss typically 10–20% due to diarrhea, anorexia, and fear of eating
'String sign' on radiographic studies indicates a severely narrowed bowel loop
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Complications and Management of Bowel Obstruction in Crohn's Disease
items.content: Bowel obstruction in Crohn's disease can manifest as intermittent symptoms due to edema and spasm or chronic obstruction from fibrostenotic narrowing. Acute episodes are often managed with intravenous fluids and gastric decompression.
keyPoints: Early bowel obstruction caused by wall edema and spasm
Chronic obstruction results from fibrostenotic narrowing
Acute episodes may be precipitated by inflammation, spasm, or impaction
Management includes intravenous fluids and gastric decompression
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Nutritional Deficiencies and Management in Jejunoileitis
items.content: Extensive inflammatory disease in the small bowel can lead to malabsorption, nutritional deficiencies, and complications such as anemia and osteoporosis. Nutrient repletion is crucial for management.
keyPoints: Malabsorption leads to steatorrhea and nutrient deficiencies
Nutritional deficiencies include anemia, hypoalbuminemia, hypocalcemia, and more
Intravenous iron often required due to poor oral tolerance
Daily multivitamin, calcium, and vitamin D supplements recommended
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Perianal Disease in Crohn's Colitis
items.content: About one-third of patients with Crohn's colitis experience perianal disease, which can manifest as incontinence, hemorrhoidal tags, anal strictures, anorectal fistulas, and perirectal abscesses. Notably, not all patients with perianal fistula show endoscopic evidence of colonic inflammation.
keyPoints: Perianal disease affects about one-third of Crohn's colitis patients
Manifestations include incontinence, hemorrhoidal tags, anal strictures, anorectal fistulas, and perirectal abscesses
Not all patients with perianal fistula have endoscopic evidence of colonic inflammation
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE (part 2)

items.title: Gastroduodenal Disease in Crohn's Disease
items.content: Symptoms of gastroduodenal disease include nausea, vomiting, and epigastric pain. Patients often have Helicobacter pylori-negative gastritis, with the second portion of the duodenum more commonly involved than the bulb. Fistulas may arise from other bowel segments without indicating upper GI tract involvement. Advanced cases can lead to chronic gastric outlet obstruction.
keyPoints: Symptoms include nausea, vomiting, and epigastric pain
Helicobacter pylori-negative gastritis is common
Second portion of the duodenum more commonly involved than the bulb
30% of children with CD have esophagogastroduodenal involvement
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE (part 2)

items.title: Diagnostic Imaging in Crohn's Disease
items.content: CT enterography and MR enterography are equally accurate for identifying active small-bowel inflammation. MRI offers superior soft tissue contrast and avoids radiation, making it preferable for younger patients. Pelvic MRI is better than CT for detecting pelvic lesions like abscesses and fistulas. Small-bowel ultrasound (SBUS) has high sensitivity and specificity for detecting small-bowel CD, especially with oral contrast medium.
keyPoints: CT enterography and MR enterography equally accurate for active small-bowel inflammation
MRI avoids radiation exposure, advantageous for younger patients
Pelvic MRI superior to CT for pelvic lesions
SBUS sensitivity 94%, specificity 97% for small-bowel CD
Oral contrast medium increases SBUS sensitivity and specificity to 100%
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: CROHN'S DISEASE (part 3) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS OF UC AND CD | INFECTIOUS DISEASES
items.title: Complications and Management of Crohn's Disease
items.content: Crohn's disease, being a transmural process, leads to complications such as fistula formation due to serosal adhesions. Perforation occurs in 1–2% of patients, primarily in the ileum. Intraabdominal and pelvic abscesses are common (10–30%) and often require CT-guided percutaneous drainage followed by resection. Systemic glucocorticoid therapy increases the risk of these abscesses.
keyPoints: Perforation occurs in 1–2% of CD patients, usually in the ileum.
Intraabdominal and pelvic abscesses occur in 10–30% of CD patients.
CT-guided percutaneous drainage is standard for abscess management; however, most require bowel resection.
Systemic glucocorticoid therapy increases risk of intraabdominal/pelvic abscesses.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE (part 3) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS OF UC AND CD | INFECTIOUS DISEASES

items.subtopic: CROHN'S DISEASE (part 3) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS OF UC AND CD | INFECTIOUS DISEASES
items.title: Differential Diagnosis Challenges Between UC and CD
items.content: Differentiating between ulcerative colitis (UC) and Crohn's disease (CD) can be challenging, with up to 15% of cases initially indeterminate. Overlapping histologic features in resection specimens make classification difficult in approximately 5% of cases.
keyPoints: Up to 15% of IBD diagnoses are initially indeterminate between UC and CD.
Approximately 5% of colon resection specimens exhibit overlapping histologic features, complicating diagnosis.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE (part 3) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS OF UC AND CD | INFECTIOUS DISEASES

items.subtopic: CROHN'S DISEASE (part 3) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS OF UC AND CD | INFECTIOUS DISEASES
items.title: Infectious Mimics of Inflammatory Bowel Disease
items.content: Several infectious diseases can mimic the symptoms and endoscopic findings of Crohn's disease or ulcerative colitis. These include bacterial infections like Campylobacter, Salmonella, Shigellosis, Yersinia, C. difficile, and E. coli; viral infections such as CMV and herpes simplex; and protozoan infections including Isospora belli and Entamoeba histolytica.
keyPoints: Campylobacter colitis can mimic severe UC.
Salmonella infection leads to watery or bloody diarrhea, with 1% becoming asymptomatic carriers.
Yersinia enterocolitica causes mucosal ulceration in the terminal ileum.
CMV and herpes simplex infections can occur even in immunocompetent individuals.
Entamoeba histolytica infects ~10% of the world's population, causing bloody diarrhea.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE (part 3) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS OF UC AND CD | INFECTIOUS DISEASES

items.title: Differentiating Crohn's Disease from Diverticulitis
items.content: Crohn's disease (CD) and diverticulitis can present similarly with fever, abdominal pain, leukocytosis, elevated ESR, partial obstruction, and fistulas. However, CD is more likely to show perianal disease or ileitis on small-bowel series, significant endoscopic mucosal abnormalities, and recurrence after segmental resection.
keyPoints: Perianal disease or ileitis favors CD diagnosis
Significant endoscopic mucosal abnormalities are more common in CD
Endoscopic or clinical recurrence following segmental resection suggests CD
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · NONINFECTIOUS DISEASES

items.title: Recognizing Ischemic Colitis and Its Mimics
items.content: Ischemic colitis can mimic inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) with chronic or segmental inflammation. It often presents suddenly in the elderly, especially post-abdominal aortic aneurysm repair or in hypercoagulable states, with left lower quadrant pain and bright red blood per rectum.
keyPoints: Consider ischemic colitis in elderly patients
Presentation: sudden onset of left lower quadrant pain and bright red blood per rectum
Endoscopic examination shows a sharp transition to inflammation in the descending colon
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · NONINFECTIOUS DISEASES

items.title: Managing ICI-Related Colitis
items.content: Immune checkpoint inhibitor (ICI)-related colitis affects 21–44% of patients, presenting with self-limited diarrhea and potential severe colitis. Management depends on symptom severity, with glucocorticoids for moderate to severe symptoms and biologics like anti-TNF agents for steroid-refractory cases.
keyPoints: ICI-related colitis affects 21–44% of patients
Clinical presentation: self-limited diarrhea potentially leading to significant morbidity
Treatment: glucocorticoids for moderate/severe symptoms, biologics for steroid-refractory cases
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · NONINFECTIOUS DISEASES

items.title: Recognizing Atypical Colitides: Collagenous and Lymphocytic Colitis
items.content: Collagenous colitis and lymphocytic colitis are atypical forms of microscopic colitis that present with normal endoscopic appearances. They are characterized by chronic watery diarrhea, increased intraepithelial lymphocytes, and distinct histologic features. Collagenous colitis involves subepithelial collagen deposition, while lymphocytic colitis does not.
keyPoints: Collagenous colitis has a female-to-male ratio of 9:1.
Most patients present in their sixth or seventh decade of life.
Risk factors include smoking, NSAIDs, proton pump inhibitors, beta blockers, and autoimmune diseases.
Lymphocytic colitis shows an equal incidence between men and women.
Sertraline use is a risk factor for lymphocytic colitis.
Celiac disease frequency in lymphocytic colitis ranges from 9 to 27%.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · THE ATYPICAL COLITIDES | EXTRAINTESTINAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Understanding Diversion Colitis and Its Management
items.content: Diversion colitis is an inflammatory condition in the colon segments not exposed to fecal stream, often seen in patients with ileostomy or colostomy. It presents with mucus or bloody discharge from the rectum and can be identified by endoscopic findings of erythema, granularity, friability, and ulceration.
keyPoints: Histopathology shows active inflammation with cryptitis and crypt abscesses but normal crypt architecture.
Differentiated from UC by normal crypt architecture; differentiation from CD is not definitive.
Short-chain fatty acid enzymes may aid in management.
Definitive therapy involves surgical reanastomosis.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · THE ATYPICAL COLITIDES | EXTRAINTESTINAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Erythema Nodosum (EN) in IBD: Correlation with Bowel Activity and Management
items.content: Erythema nodosum is a dermatologic manifestation associated with inflammatory bowel disease, occurring more frequently in Crohn's Disease (CD) than Ulcerative Colitis (UC). EN lesions correlate with bowel activity, typically developing after the onset of bowel symptoms. They present as hot, red, tender nodules on the lower extremities and are managed by treating the underlying bowel condition.
keyPoints: EN occurs in up to 15% of CD patients and 10% of UC patients.
Lesions correlate with bowel activity, appearing after onset of bowel symptoms.
Typical lesion locations: anterior surface of lower legs, ankles, calves, thighs, arms.
Management focuses on the underlying bowel disease.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · DERMATOLOGIC

items.title: Pyoderma Gangrenosum (PG) in IBD: Presentation and Treatment Challenges
items.content: Pyoderma gangrenosis is a severe dermatologic condition associated with inflammatory bowel disease, particularly Ulcerative Colitis. It can present independently of bowel symptoms or even precede them by years. PG lesions often start as pustules that spread and ulcerate, forming large, necrotic ulcers. Treatment is challenging and may require systemic therapies including IV antibiotics, glucocorticoids, immunosuppressants, and biologics.
keyPoints: PG occurs in 1–12% of UC patients and less commonly in Crohn's colitis.
Lesions can appear before or independently from bowel symptoms.
Typical lesion progression: pustule to ulcer with necrotic tissue, violaceous edges, surrounded by erythema.
Treatment may involve IV antibiotics, glucocorticoids, dapsone, azathioprine, thalidomide, CSA, infliximab, or adalimumab.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · DERMATOLOGIC

items.title: Peripheral Arthritis in IBD: Correlation with Bowel Activity and Treatment
items.content: Peripheral arthritis occurs in 15–20% of IBD patients, more commonly in Crohn's Disease (CD), and its severity correlates with bowel activity. It is asymmetric, polyarticular, migratory, and affects large joints. Management focuses on reducing bowel inflammation; severe Ulcerative Colitis (UC) may be cured by colectomy.
keyPoints: Peripheral arthritis occurs in 15–20% of IBD patients.
More common in CD than UC.
Worsens with exacerbations of bowel activity.
Affects large joints asymmetrically and migratorily.
Treatment targets reducing bowel inflammation; colectomy may cure severe UC.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · RHEUMATOLOGIC | OCULAR | HEPATOBILIARY

items.title: Ankylosing Spondylitis in IBD: Association with HLA-B27 and Treatment Implications
items.content: Ankylosing spondylitis (AS) is found in ~10% of IBD patients, more frequently in CD than UC. Approximately two-thirds express the HLA-B27 antigen. AS activity does not correlate with bowel activity and remains unaffected by glucocorticoids or colectomy. It primarily affects the spine and pelvis, leading to progressive skeletal damage. Anti-TNF therapy is effective for reducing spinal inflammation.
keyPoints: AS occurs in ~10% of IBD patients, more common in CD than UC.
~Two-thirds express HLA-B27 antigen.
Activity not related to bowel activity; unaffected by glucocorticoids or colectomy.
Affects spine and pelvis causing progressive skeletal damage.
Anti-TNF therapy reduces spinal inflammation.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · RHEUMATOLOGIC | OCULAR | HEPATOBILIARY

items.title: Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis (PSC) in IBD: Diagnosis and Prognosis
items.content: PSC is associated with ~5% of UC patients but 50–75% of PSC patients have IBD. It involves bile duct inflammation and fibrosis, potentially leading to cirrhosis and hepatic failure over 5–10 years. MRCP is a safe initial diagnostic test; ERCP remains the gold standard. Gallbladder polyps in PSC patients often indicate malignancy, necessitating cholecystectomy even for lesions <1 cm.
keyPoints: ~5% of UC patients have PSC; 50–75% of PSC patients have IBD.
Involves bile duct inflammation and fibrosis leading to cirrhosis/hepatic failure over 5–10 years.
MRCP is a safe initial diagnostic test; ERCP is the gold standard.
Gallbladder polyps in PSC often malignant, cholecystectomy recommended for lesions <1 cm.
PSC patients have a 10–15% lifetime risk of developing cholangiocarcinoma.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · RHEUMATOLOGIC | OCULAR | HEPATOBILIARY

items.title: Understanding Nephrolithiasis in Crohn's Disease Patients Post-Small-Bowel Resection
items.content: Nephrolithiasis is a common genitourinary complication in patients with Crohn's disease (CD) following small-bowel resection, occurring in 10–20% of cases. This condition arises due to hyperoxaluria, where increased absorption of dietary oxalate leads to calcium oxalate stone formation.
keyPoints: Nephrolithiasis frequency: 10-20% in CD patients post-small-bowel resection
Mechanism: Hyperoxaluria from increased dietary oxalate absorption
Pathophysiology: In ileal dysfunction, fatty acids bind calcium, leaving oxalate unbound and absorbed in the colon
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · UROLOGIC | METABOLIC BONE DISORDERS

items.title: Risk Factors and Management of Osteoporosis in IBD Patients
items.content: IBD patients have a 14–42% prevalence of low bone mass, with increased risks from glucocorticoids, CSA, MTX, TPN, malabsorption, and inflammation. Fracture rates are notably high, especially in the elderly, with an annual osteoporotic fracture risk of ~1%. Chronic glucocorticoid use can lead to significant bone loss.
keyPoints: Low bone mass prevalence: 14-42% in IBD patients
Fracture incidence: 36% in CD and 45% in UC
Annual osteoporotic fracture risk: ~1%
Fracture rates highest in elderly (age >60)
Odds ratios for fractures: OR of 1.72 for vertebral, 1.59 for hip
13% of IBD patients with fractures on antifactor treatment
Bone mass loss from glucocorticoids: up to 20% per year
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · UROLOGIC | METABOLIC BONE DISORDERS

items.title: Increased Thromboembolic Risk in IBD Patients
items.content: Patients with inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) have an elevated risk of thromboembolic events, including both venous and arterial thrombosis, regardless of whether the disease is active. This increased risk is due to a variety of factors contributing to a hypercoagulable state.
keyPoints: Factors include abnormalities in platelet-endothelial interaction, hyperhomocysteinemia, alterations in coagulation cascade, impaired fibrinolysis, tissue factor-bearing microvesicles, autoantibodies disrupting normal coagulation, and genetic predisposition.
A spectrum of vasculitides affecting small, medium, and large vessels is also observed.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · THROMBOEMBOLIC DISORDERS | OTHER DISORDERS | 5-ASA AGENTS

items.title: Cardiopulmonary Manifestations in IBD Patients
items.content: IBD patients may experience various cardiopulmonary manifestations, including endocarditis, myocarditis, pleuropericarditis, and interstitial lung disease. Secondary or reactive amyloidosis can also occur, particularly in those with long-standing Crohn's Disease (CD).
keyPoints: Amyloid material deposition leads to symptoms like diarrhea, constipation, and renal failure.
Renal involvement from amyloidosis can be treated with colchicine.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · THROMBOEMBOLIC DISORDERS | OTHER DISORDERS | 5-ASA AGENTS

items.title: Efficacy and Side Effects of 5-ASA Agents in UC Treatment
items.content: 5-ASA agents are effective for inducing and maintaining remission in ulcerative colitis (UC). Various formulations, including sulfasalazine, balsalazide, and mesalamine preparations like Lialda and Apriso, offer different release mechanisms and dosing schedules.
keyPoints: Sulfasalazine is effective for mild to moderate UC but has a high rate of side effects at doses ≥6 g/d, including hypersensitivity reactions.
50–75% of patients with mild to moderate UC improve with 5-ASA doses equivalent to 2 g/d of mesalamine; dose response up to 4.8 g/d.
Common side effects include headaches, nausea, hair loss, and abdominal pain; rare ones include renal impairment, hematuria, pancreatitis, and paradoxical worsening of colitis.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · THROMBOEMBOLIC DISORDERS | OTHER DISORDERS | 5-ASA AGENTS

items.title: Use of Glucocorticoids in UC and CD: Dosing, Efficacy, and Side Effects
items.content: Glucocorticoids are effective for moderate to severe ulcerative colitis (UC) and Crohn's disease (CD). Prednisone is initiated at 40–60 mg/d for UC unresponsive to 5-ASA therapy. Parenteral options include hydrocortisone (300 mg/d) or methylprednisolone (40–60 mg/d). Budesonide, with minimal side effects and released in the colon, is prescribed at 9 mg/d for 8 weeks without tapering. Topical glucocorticoids are beneficial for distal colitis but can cause adrenal suppression if absorbed from the rectum. In CD, controlled-ileal-release budesonide matches prednisone efficacy with fewer side effects and is used for 2–3 months at 9 mg/d before tapering.
keyPoints: Prednisone dosed at 40–60 mg/d for UC unresponsive to 5-ASA therapy
Parenteral glucocorticoids: hydrocortisone 300 mg/d or methylprednisolone 40–60 mg/d
Budesonide (Uceris) dose is 9 mg/d for 8 weeks, no taper required
Topical glucocorticoids can lead to adrenal suppression if absorbed from the rectum
Controlled-ileal-release budesonide used at 9 mg/d for 2–3 months in CD
Glucocorticoids have numerous side effects including fluid retention, hyperglycemia, and osteonecrosis
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · GLUCOCORTICOIDS | ANTIBIOTICS

items.title: Role of Antibiotics in UC and Pouchitis Management
items.content: Antibiotics do not play a role in treating active or quiescent ulcerative colitis (UC). However, they are effective for pouchitis, which affects 30–50% of UC patients post-colectomy with ileal pouch anal anastomosis (IPAA). Pouchitis typically responds to antibiotics like metronidazole and ciprofloxacin, with some cases requiring long-term antibiotic therapy.
keyPoints: Antibiotics have no role in active or quiescent UC treatment
Pouchitis occurs in 30–50% of UC patients post-IPAA
Effective antibiotics for pouchitis include metronidazole and ciprofloxacin
Some patients with chronic pouchitis require long-term antibiotic therapy
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · GLUCOCORTICOIDS | ANTIBIOTICS

items.title: Monitoring and Adjusting Therapy in Azathioprine and Mercaptopurine Use
items.content: Azathioprine and mercaptopurine are purine analogues used to inhibit purine ribonucleotide synthesis, affecting cell proliferation. Monitoring therapy efficacy and adjusting doses can be guided by measuring metabolite levels (6-thioguanine and 6-methylmercaptopurine) or using weight-based dosing.
keyPoints: Efficacy may appear in 3–4 weeks but up to 4–6 months.
Doses: Azathioprine 2-3 mg/kg/day, MP 1-1.5 mg/kg/day.
Adherence monitored by metabolite levels (6-thioguanine and 6-methylmercaptopurine).
Regular CBC monitoring due to dose-related bone marrow suppression.
Thiopurine methyltransferase deficiency affects 1 in 300, increasing toxicity risk.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · AZATHIOPRINE AND MERCAPTOPURINE

items.title: Risk of Lymphoma and Skin Cancer with Thiopurines
items.content: Thiopurine therapy is associated with an increased risk of lymphoma, particularly in patients over 65 years old. There's also a heightened risk for nonmelanoma skin cancers.
keyPoints: Fourfold risk of lymphoma in IBD patients on thiopurines.
Highest lymphoma risk in >65-year-olds (incidence rate: 5.41 per 1000 patient-years).
Moderate risk for ages 50-65 (incidence rate: 2.58 per 1000 patient-years).
Lowest risk in <50-year-olds (incidence rate: 0.37 per 1000 patient-years).
Two-to-threefold increased risk of nonmelanoma skin cancers.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · AZATHIOPRINE AND MERCAPTOPURINE

items.title: MTX and CSA in IBD: Mechanisms, Uses, and Monitoring
items.content: Methotrexate (MTX) inhibits dihydrofolate reductase, impairing DNA synthesis and reducing IL-1 production, used with biologics to enhance response. Cyclosporine (CSA) blocks T-cell activation by inhibiting calcineurin via cyclophilin binding, effective in steroid-refractory UC at 2–4 mg/kg/day IV.
keyPoints: MTX: Inhibits dihydrofolate reductase; used with biologics; IM/SC doses: 15-25 mg/week; monitor CBCs and liver enzymes for leukopenia/hepatic fibrosis; rare hypersensitivity pneumonitis.

CSA: Blocks IL-2 production by T helper cells via calcineurin inhibition; effective in severe UC (82% response at 2–4 mg/kg/day IV); maintain levels between 150-350 ng/mL; monitor renal function and watch for hypertension, gingival hyperplasia, tremors, etc.; prophylaxis with trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole recommended.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · METHOTREXATE | CYCLOSPORINE

items.title: Toxicity and Monitoring in MTX and CSA Therapy for IBD
items.content: MTX therapy requires monitoring of CBCs and liver enzymes due to risks of leukopenia and hepatic fibrosis, with liver biopsy considered in cases of elevated liver enzymes. CSA therapy necessitates frequent renal function checks and serum level maintenance between 150-350 ng/mL; dose adjustments are needed for creatinine elevation.
keyPoints: MTX: Monitor CBCs and liver enzymes regularly; consider liver biopsy if liver enzymes increase; hypersensitivity pneumonitis is a rare complication.

CSA: Renal function monitoring essential; maintain serum levels 150-350 ng/mL; adjust dose for creatinine elevation (>3.1 mmol/L); prophylaxis against Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia with trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · METHOTREXATE | CYCLOSPORINE

items.title: Tacrolimus: Effective for Refractory IBD Despite Small-Bowel Involvement
items.content: Tacrolimus is a potent immunomodulatory agent effective in treating refractory IBD, particularly beneficial in cases with proximal small-bowel Crohn's disease due to its good oral absorption.
keyPoints: 100 times as potent as CSA
Not dependent on bile or mucosal integrity for absorption
Effective in children with refractory IBD and adults with extensive small bowel involvement
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · TACROLIMUS | BIOLOGIC THERAPIES

items.title: Biologic Therapies: Early Aggressive Treatment in High-Risk IBD Patients
items.content: Biologics are recommended as initial therapy for moderate to severe CD and UC, especially in high-risk patients, aiming for deep remission by combining immunomodulators with biologics.
keyPoints: Given initially for moderate to severe CD and UC
High-risk UC: steroid-dependent/refractory disease, refractory pouchitis
High-risk CD: <30 years, extensive disease, perianal/severe rectal disease
Goals: early treatment, aggressive therapy, dual therapy, deep remission
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · TACROLIMUS | BIOLOGIC THERAPIES

items.title: Anti-TNF Therapies: Combination and Dosing Strategies for IBD
items.content: Combination therapy with infliximab and azathioprine is more effective than monotherapy in moderate to severe CD and UC. Adalimumab shows better results when dosed at 40 mg weekly.
keyPoints: Infliximab: first biologic approved for moderately to severely active CD and UC
Combination therapy with infliximab and azathioprine is more effective than monotherapy
Adalimumab remission rates increase with a dose of 40 mg weekly
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · TACROLIMUS | BIOLOGIC THERAPIES

items.title: Antibody Development and Infusion Reactions
items.content: The development of antibodies to infliximab increases the risk of infusion reactions and decreases treatment response. Commercial assays can detect these antibodies and measure trough levels, aiding in optimal dosing decisions.
keyPoints: Development of anti-infliximab antibodies is linked with increased infusion reactions.
Decreased response to infliximab correlates with antibody presence.
High anti-infliximab antibodies and low trough level suggest switching to another anti-TNF therapy.
Addition of an immunomodulator (e.g., azathioprine, MP, MTX) can help prevent antibody formation.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · Side Effects of Anti-TNF Therapies

items.title: Risk of Lymphoma with Anti-TNF Therapy
items.content: The baseline risk of NHL in CD patients is slightly higher than the general population. While azathioprine and/or MP therapy increases this risk, no excess lymphoma risk was found for anti-TNF medications after adjusting for co-treatments.
keyPoints: Baseline NHL risk in CD patients: 2 in 10,000.
Risk with azathioprine/MP therapy: ~4 in 10,000.
No excess lymphoma risk from anti-TNF meds after adjustment (Danish study).
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · Side Effects of Anti-TNF Therapies

items.title: Increased Risk of Skin Lesions and Melanoma
items.content: Anti-TNF therapy can lead to new-onset psoriasiform skin lesions in nearly 5% of IBD patients, with an almost twofold increased risk of melanoma. Regular skin checks are recommended for those on these medications.
keyPoints: New-onset psoriasiform skin lesions occur in ~5% of anti-TNF treated IBD patients.
Risk of melanoma is nearly doubled with anti-TNF therapy, not thiopurines.
Annual skin check recommended for patients on these therapies.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · Side Effects of Anti-TNF Therapies

items.title: Vedolizumab: Gut-Selective Immunosuppression in IBD
items.content: Vedolizumab is a monoclonal antibody targeting $\alpha4\beta7$-integrin, selectively inhibiting gut-homing T lymphocytes' adhesion to MAdCAM-1. This specificity allows for effective reduction of GI inflammation without systemic immunosuppression or CNS effects.
keyPoints: Vedolizumab is a monoclonal antibody against $\alpha4\beta7$-integrin, providing gut-selective immunosuppression.
It inhibits adhesion of T lymphocytes to MAdCAM-1 but not Vascular Adhesion Molecule 1.
May be prescribed as first-line biologic or after TNF antagonist failure in CD or UC.
More effective than adalimumab for first-line therapy in moderate to severe UC.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · ANTI-INTEGRINS

items.title: Risankizumab: Selective IL-23 Inhibition for IBD
items.content: Risankizumab is a monoclonal antibody that selectively binds the IL-23 p19 subunit, inhibiting its interaction with the IL-23 receptor complex. It offers a targeted treatment option for moderate to severe CD, especially in patients who have failed other advanced therapies.
keyPoints: Risankizumab is the first selective IL-23 inhibitor approved for moderate to severe CD.
It selectively binds to the IL-23 p19 subunit, inhibiting interaction with IL-23R complex.
Provides an additional therapeutic option for patients previously treated with other advanced IBD therapies.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · ANTI-INTEGRINS

items.title: Mechanism of Action and Clinical Use of JAK Inhibitors in IBD
items.content: JAK inhibitors, such as tofacitinib and upadacitinib, are small molecules that inhibit the JAK/STAT pathway, reducing cytokine production and altering immune responses. Tofacitinib is a pan-JAK inhibitor with higher specificity for JAK1 and JAK3, while upadacitinib selectively inhibits JAK1 more potently than other isoforms.
keyPoints: JAK inhibitors inhibit the JAK/STAT pathway involved in IBD pathogenesis.
Tofacitinib is a pan-JAK inhibitor with higher specificity for JAK1 and JAK3.
Upadacitinib selectively inhibits JAK1 more potently than JAK2, JAK3, and TYK2.
Both are FDA approved as second-line agents after anti-TNF therapy failure.
Increased risk of serious adverse events including heart attack, stroke, cancer, blood clots, and death with JAK inhibitors.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · SMALL MOLECULES

items.title: Safety Considerations and Indications for Ozanimod in IBD
items.content: Ozanimod is a sphingosine-1-phosphate (S1P1) receptor modulator that selectively binds to S1P1 and S1P5 receptors, preventing lymphocyte trafficking from lymph nodes to the gut. It is approved for moderate to severe ulcerative colitis.
keyPoints: Ozanimod binds selectively to S1P1 and S1P5 receptor subtypes.
It prevents disease-exacerbating lymphocytes from reaching the gut.
Approved for moderate to severe ulcerative colitis as a daily capsule.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · SMALL MOLECULES

items.title: Exclusive Enteral Nutrition (EEN) for Inducing Remission in Crohn's Disease
items.content: Exclusive enteral nutrition (EEN) or bowel rest with total parenteral nutrition (TPN) can be as effective as glucocorticoids in inducing remission of active Crohn's disease (CD). However, these interventions are not suitable for maintenance therapy.
keyPoints: EEN and TPN are effective as glucocorticoids in inducing remission of active CD
Not effective for maintenance therapy in CD
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · NUTRITIONAL THERAPIES

items.title: Dietary Management Differences Between Crohn's Disease (CD) and Ulcerative Colitis (UC)
items.content: Active ulcerative colitis (UC) does not respond effectively to elemental diets or total parenteral nutrition (TPN), unlike active CD, which can be managed with exclusive enteral nutrition (EEN) or TPN.
keyPoints: Elemental diets and TPN are ineffective for active UC
Dietary management strategies differ between CD and UC
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · NUTRITIONAL THERAPIES

items.title: Surgical Indications and Outcomes in Ulcerative Colitis
items.content: Nearly half of patients with extensive chronic ulcerative colitis (UC) undergo surgery within the first 10 years. The operation of choice is an ileal pouch-anal anastomosis (IPAA), which preserves anal sphincter function and maintains continence. However, complications include a 5–10% risk of pouch failure necessitating permanent ileostomy.
keyPoints: Nearly one-half of patients with extensive chronic UC undergo surgery within the first 10 years.
The operation of choice is an IPAA, preserving anal sphincter function and maintaining continence.
Pouch failure necessitating conversion to a permanent ileostomy occurs in 5–10% of patients.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · SURGICAL THERAPY

items.title: Management and Complications of IPAA in UC
items.content: Patients with an IPAA typically experience ~6–10 bowel movements per day. Pouchitis, occurring in 30–50% of patients, is the most frequent complication and can be managed with antibiotics, though 3–5% may require chronic therapy or pouch removal.
keyPoints: Patients with IPAA usually have ~6–10 bowel movements a day.
Pouchitis occurs in ~30–50% of patients with UC post-IPAA.
3–5% of patients with pouchitis remain refractory and may require chronic antibiotic therapy or pouch removal.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · SURGICAL THERAPY

items.title: Surgical Considerations in Crohn's Disease
items.content: Most patients with Crohn's disease (CD) will need surgery, especially those with small-bowel involvement (80% chance). Surgery is indicated when medical treatment fails or complications arise. Risk factors for early recurrence include smoking and penetrating disease.
keyPoints: The majority of CD patients require at least one operation in their lifetime.
Patients with small-bowel disease have an 80% chance of requiring surgery.
Risk factors for early recurrence include cigarette smoking, penetrating disease, and multiple surgeries.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · SURGICAL THERAPY

items.title: Timing and Safety of Conception in IBD Patients
items.content: Patients with quiescent ulcerative colitis (UC) or Crohn's disease (CD) should aim for remission 3–6 months before conception to optimize birth outcomes. The course of UC or CD during pregnancy generally correlates with the disease activity at conception.
keyPoints: Remission for 3–6 months recommended before conceiving
Mild or quiescent UC/CD does not affect birth outcomes
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · IBD AND PREGNANCY

items.title: Delivery Considerations in IBD Patients with IPAA
items.content: UC patients with an ileal pouch-anal anastomosis (IPAA) may consider cesarean delivery to reduce the risk of future fecal incontinence, especially if they do not desire multiple children. Most CD patients can deliver vaginally unless complications like abscesses or fistulas are present.
keyPoints: Cesarean delivery preferred for UC with IPAA to prevent fecal incontinence
Vaginal delivery is usually possible for CD patients
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · IBD AND PREGNANCY

items.title: Medication Safety and Management During Pregnancy
items.content: Sulfasalazine, mesalamines, glucocorticoids, ampicillin, cephalosporins, and metronidazole are generally safe during pregnancy. MTX should be discontinued 3 months before conception due to teratogenicity. Biologics like infliximab and ADA are safe but can affect infants' serum levels; live vaccines should be avoided until 12 months of age.
keyPoints: Sulfasalazine, mesalamines, glucocorticoids are safe during pregnancy
MTX is teratogenic; discontinue at least 3 months before conception
Biologics like infliximab and ADA cross the placenta; avoid live vaccines until 12 months
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · IBD AND PREGNANCY

items.title: Surveillance Strategy for Colorectal Cancer Risk in UC and CD
items.content: Patients with long-standing ulcerative colitis (UC) or Crohn's disease (CD) have an increased risk of developing colorectal cancer. The risk is influenced by the duration and extent of the disease, with surveillance strategies recommended to mitigate this risk.
keyPoints: Risk of neoplasia in UC increases over time: 2% after 10 years, 8% after 20 years, 18% after 30 years.
Colorectal cancer rates are about 1.5–2 times higher than the general population for those with chronic UC.
Annual or biennial colonoscopy recommended for patients with >8-10 years of extensive colitis or 12-15 years of proctosigmoiditis.
Chromoendoscopy is recommended for dysplasia surveillance in IBD, especially in higher-risk patients.
In the CESAME study, standardized incidence ratios of colorectal cancer were 2.2 for all IBD patients and 7.0 for those with long-standing extensive colitis.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · ULCERATIVE COLITIS | CROHN'S DISEASE

items.title: Risk Factors and Management of Colorectal Cancer in Crohn's Disease
items.content: Crohn's disease (CD) patients, particularly those with long-duration and extensive colitis, have an elevated risk for colorectal cancer. Surveillance strategies similar to UC are recommended due to comparable risks.
keyPoints: Risk factors for colorectal cancer in CD include long-duration, extensive disease, bypassed colon segments, strictures, PSC, and family history of colon cancer.
Cancer risks in CD and UC are likely equivalent for similar extent and duration of disease.
Endoscopic surveillance strategy used for UC is recommended for chronic Crohn's colitis.
A pediatric colonoscope can be used to pass narrow strictures in CD patients; surgery should be considered for symptomatic patients with impassable strictures.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · ULCERATIVE COLITIS | CROHN'S DISEASE

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF DYSPLASIA AND CANCER | COVID-19 AND IBD
items.title: Management of Dysplasia in Inflammatory Bowel Disease
items.content: In patients with inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), dysplasia management varies based on the type and location. High-grade flat dysplasia typically necessitates colectomy for ulcerative colitis (UC) and either colectomy or segmental resection for Crohn's disease (CD). Low-grade flat dysplasia often leads to immediate colectomy, while adenomas can be endoscopically removed if surrounding mucosa is free of dysplasia.
keyPoints: High-grade flat dysplasia in UC usually requires colectomy.
For CD, high-grade flat dysplasia may lead to either colectomy or segmental resection.
Low-grade flat dysplasia generally results in immediate colectomy.
Adenomas can be endoscopically removed if surrounding mucosa is free of dysplasia.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · MANAGEMENT OF DYSPLASIA AND CANCER | COVID-19 AND IBD

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF DYSPLASIA AND CANCER | COVID-19 AND IBD
items.title: COVID-19 Risk Factors in IBD Patients
items.content: IBD patients on immunosuppressive therapy may face increased risk of severe COVID-19. Key factors include age, comorbidities, and systemic glucocorticoid use. Anti-TNF treatment does not significantly affect the severity of COVID-19.
keyPoints: Increasing age is associated with severe COVID-19 (adjusted OR 1.04; 95% CI, 1.01–1.02).
Two or more comorbidities increase risk (adjusted OR 2.9; 95% CI, 1.1–7.8).
Systemic glucocorticoids significantly raise the risk (adjusted OR 6.9; 95% CI, 2.3–20.5).
Anti-TNF treatment is not associated with severe COVID-19 (adjusted OR 0.9; 95% CI, 0.4–2.2).
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · MANAGEMENT OF DYSPLASIA AND CANCER | COVID-19 AND IBD

items.explanation: The passage mentions that only ~20% of the disease risk is considered to be explained by the current genetic information.

items.question: Which genetic approach has identified approximately 240 genetic loci associated with IBD?
items.explanation: The passage indicates that GWAS have identified ~240 genetic loci associated with IBD.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a gene involved in host-commensal interactions related to IBD?
items.explanation: The passage lists IL10, CTLA4, and NCF2 as genes involved in host-commensal interactions but does not mention BRCA1.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS

items.question: Which factor is NOT mentioned as influencing the establishment and maintenance of intestinal microbiota composition?
items.explanation: The passage mentions immune, environmental (diet and antibiotics), and genetic factors but not physical exercise.

items.question: What is the approximate biomass of bacteria achieved by age 3 in grams of feces?
items.explanation: The passage states a biomass of $\sim10^{12}$ colony-forming units per gram is achieved by age 3.

items.question: Which microorganism is mentioned as potentially protective against inflammation in the context of IBD?
items.explanation: The passage mentions Faecalibacterium prausnitzii as a microorganism that may hinder inflammation.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · COMMENSAL MICROBIOTA AND IBD

items.question: Which cytokine is NOT mentioned as being secreted by CD4+ T regulatory cells involved in inducing oral tolerance?
items.explanation: The passage mentions IL-10, IL-35, and TGF-$\beta$ as cytokines secreted by CD4+ T regulatory cells but does not mention IFN-$\gamma$.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · DEFECTIVE IMMUNE REGULATION IN IBD

items.question: What is a consequence of defective immune regulation in IBD according to the passage?
items.explanation: The passage states that in IBD, the suppression of inflammation is altered, leading to uncontrolled inflammation.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · DEFECTIVE IMMUNE REGULATION IN IBD

items.question: Which factor is NOT listed as being involved in inducing oral tolerance?
options: Deletion or anergy of antigen-reactive T cells
Induction of CD4+ T regulatory cells
items.explanation: The passage mentions the induction of anti-inflammatory cytokines, not pro-inflammatory ones, in inducing oral tolerance.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · DEFECTIVE IMMUNE REGULATION IN IBD

items.question: Which cytokine is primarily responsible for initiating the T_H1 cytokine pathway in experimental models of mucosal inflammation?
items.explanation: IL-12 is a key cytokine in the pathogenesis of experimental models of mucosal inflammation, initiating the T_H1 pathway.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · THE INFLAMMATORY CASCADE IN IBD

items.question: Which type of cells are associated with promoting fibrogenesis and collagen production in IBD?
items.explanation: Inflammatory cytokines from innate immune cells such as IL-1, IL-6, IL-12, IL-23, and TNF promote fibrogenesis and collagen production.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · THE INFLAMMATORY CASCADE IN IBD

items.question: What is the role of sphingosine-1-phosphate (S1P) gradients in lymphocyte movement?
options: They influence lymphocyte emigration from lymph nodes.
items.explanation: S1P gradients act on S1P receptors to influence the movement of lymphocytes such as CD4+ T_H cells out of the lymph nodes.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · THE INFLAMMATORY CASCADE IN IBD

items.question: Which therapeutic approach targets the signaling by proinflammatory cytokine receptors in IBD?
items.explanation: Therapies such as anti-TNF antibodies target the signaling by proinflammatory cytokine receptors.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · THE INFLAMMATORY CASCADE IN IBD

items.question: Which histologic feature in ulcerative colitis helps distinguish it from infectious or acute self-limited colitis?
options: Crypt architecture is distorted with bifid crypts and reduced number.
Presence of villous atrophy in the colon.
Absence of neutrophils in the epithelium.
Complete involvement of deeper layers of the bowel wall.
items.explanation: Distorted crypt architecture with bifid and reduced number of crypts is a chronic feature distinguishing UC from acute conditions.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · PATHOLOGY | ULCERATIVE COLITIS: MICROSCOPIC FEATURES

items.question: In ulcerative colitis, where does the inflammatory process primarily occur?
options: Throughout all layers of the bowel wall.
Limited to the mucosa and superficial submucosa.
Primarily in the muscularis propria layer.
Only in the deep submucosal layers.
items.explanation: The inflammation is limited to the mucosa and superficial submucosa, except in fulminant disease where deeper layers are affected.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · PATHOLOGY | ULCERATIVE COLITIS: MICROSCOPIC FEATURES

items.question: What histologic finding may be present in patients with long-standing ulcerative colitis?
options: Basal plasma cells and multiple basal lymphoid aggregates.
Complete absence of inflammatory cell infiltrate.
items.explanation: Long-standing UC may present with basal plasma cells and multiple basal lymphoid aggregates as chronic histologic features.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · PATHOLOGY | ULCERATIVE COLITIS: MICROSCOPIC FEATURES

items.question: Which part of the gastrointestinal tract is most frequently involved in Crohn's disease?
items.explanation: The terminal ileum is involved in 90% of patients with small-intestinal Crohn's disease.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE: MACROSCOPIC FEATURES | CROHN'S DISEASE: MICROSCOPIC FEATURES

items.subtopic: CROHN'S DISEASE: MACROSCOPIC FEATURES | CROHN'S DISEASE: MICROSCOPIC FEATURES
items.question: What endoscopic feature is characteristic of mild Crohn's disease?
items.explanation: Mild Crohn's disease is characterized by aphthous or small superficial ulcerations.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE: MACROSCOPIC FEATURES | CROHN'S DISEASE: MICROSCOPIC FEATURES

items.question: Which histologic feature is a characteristic of Crohn's disease?
items.explanation: Noncaseating granulomas are a characteristic histologic feature of Crohn's disease.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE: MACROSCOPIC FEATURES | CROHN'S DISEASE: MICROSCOPIC FEATURES

items.question: Which symptom is most commonly associated with proctitis in ulcerative colitis?
options: Passage of fresh blood or blood-stained mucus
items.explanation: Patients with proctitis usually pass fresh blood or blood-stained mucus, either mixed with stool or streaked onto the surface of a normal or hard stool.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · ULCERATIVE COLITIS

items.question: What is a common physical sign observed during rectal examination in patients with proctitis?
items.explanation: Physical signs of proctitis include a tender anal canal and blood on rectal examination.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · ULCERATIVE COLITIS

items.question: Which laboratory marker is highly sensitive and specific for detecting intestinal inflammation in ulcerative colitis?
items.explanation: Fecal lactoferrin, a glycoprotein present in activated neutrophils, is a highly sensitive and specific marker for detecting intestinal inflammation.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · ULCERATIVE COLITIS

items.question: What endoscopic feature characterizes severe disease activity in ulcerative colitis?
items.explanation: Endoscopically, severe disease is characterized by spontaneous bleeding and ulcerations.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · ULCERATIVE COLITIS

items.question: Which clinical manifestation is most commonly associated with ileocolitis in Crohn's disease?
options: Recurrent episodes of right lower quadrant pain and diarrhea
Weight gain due to increased appetite
items.explanation: Ileocolitis often presents with a chronic history of recurrent episodes of right lower quadrant pain and diarrhea.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE (part 1)

items.question: What does the 'string sign' on radiographic studies indicate in Crohn's disease?
options: A severely narrowed loop of bowel resembling a frayed cotton string
Complete obstruction of the bowel lumen
items.explanation: The 'string sign' results from a severely narrowed loop of bowel, making it resemble a frayed cotton string.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE (part 1)

items.question: Which condition can result from severe inflammation in the ileocecal region?
options: Localized wall thinning with microperforation and fistula formation
Complete resolution of symptoms without treatment
items.explanation: Severe inflammation in the ileocecal region may lead to localized wall thinning, with microperforation and fistula formation.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE (part 1)

items.question: What is a common nutritional deficiency associated with jejunoileitis in Crohn's disease?
items.explanation: Jejunoileitis can cause anemia and hypocalcemia due to malabsorption.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE (part 1)

items.question: Which laboratory marker is more sensitive for detecting ileocolonic or colonic inflammation in Crohn's disease?
items.explanation: Fecal calprotectin is a more sensitive marker of ileocolonic or colonic inflammation rather than isolated ileal inflammation.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE (part 2)

items.question: What is the typical response to endoscopic dilation for strictures in Crohn's disease?
options: Strictures ≤4 cm and at anastomotic sites respond better
items.explanation: Strictures ≤4 cm in length and those at anastomotic sites respond better to endoscopic dilation.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE (part 2)

items.question: Which imaging modality is preferred for avoiding radiation exposure when monitoring Crohn's disease?
items.explanation: MRI offers superior soft tissue contrast and avoids radiation exposure, making it preferable for monitoring therapy.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE (part 2)

items.question: What is the sensitivity of small-bowel ultrasound when detecting lesions in Crohn's disease?
items.explanation: Small-bowel ultrasound has a sensitivity of 94% for detecting small-bowel CD.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE (part 2)

items.explanation: Intestinal obstruction occurs in 40% of patients with Crohn's disease.

items.question: What is the standard therapy for intraabdominal and pelvic abscesses in Crohn's disease patients?
items.explanation: CT-guided percutaneous drainage is the standard therapy for intraabdominal and pelvic abscesses in Crohn's disease.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE (part 3) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS OF UC AND CD | INFECTIOUS DISEASES

items.question: Which infectious agent can cause a relapse of established ulcerative colitis?
items.explanation: Campylobacter colitis can mimic the endoscopic appearance of severe ulcerative colitis and cause a relapse.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE (part 3) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS OF UC AND CD | INFECTIOUS DISEASES
