items.question: Which serologic marker is mentioned as having unclear clinical usefulness due to varying predictive values?
items.explanation: The clinical usefulness of ASCA and pANCA is unclear due to varying positive and negative predictive values.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE (part 3) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS OF UC AND CD | INFECTIOUS DISEASES

items.question: Which of the following features is more likely to be observed in Crohn's disease (CD) than in diverticulitis?
options: Perianal disease or ileitis on small-bowel series
items.explanation: Perianal disease or ileitis on small-bowel series favors the diagnosis of CD over diverticulitis.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · NONINFECTIOUS DISEASES

items.question: What is a common clinical presentation of ischemic colitis?
options: Left lower quadrant pain with bright red blood per rectum
Right upper quadrant pain with jaundice
items.explanation: Ischemic colitis commonly presents with sudden onset of left lower quadrant pain, urgency to defecate, and passage of bright red blood per rectum.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · NONINFECTIOUS DISEASES

items.question: Which condition can present with bloody, mucoid diarrhea and tenesmus when the rectum and sigmoid are irradiated?
items.explanation: Radiotherapy of the rectum and sigmoid can cause symptoms similar to distal UC, including bloody, mucoid diarrhea and tenesmus.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · NONINFECTIOUS DISEASES

items.question: What is a common feature in patients with ICI-related colitis?
options: Self-limited diarrhea that can lead to significant morbidity and mortality if not managed
Rectal ulceration 3–15 cm from the anal verge
Diarrhea and abdominal pain due to NSAID use
items.explanation: ICI-related colitis often presents with self-limited diarrhea that can lead to significant morbidity and mortality if not managed appropriately.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · NONINFECTIOUS DISEASES

items.question: Which histologic component is characteristic of collagenous colitis but not lymphocytic colitis?
items.explanation: Collagenous colitis is characterized by increased subepithelial collagen deposition, unlike lymphocytic colitis.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · THE ATYPICAL COLITIDES | EXTRAINTESTINAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: Which medication is a risk factor for lymphocytic colitis but not for collagenous colitis?
items.explanation: Use of sertraline is a risk factor specifically mentioned for lymphocytic colitis.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · THE ATYPICAL COLITIDES | EXTRAINTESTINAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: What is the definitive therapy for diversion colitis?
items.explanation: The definitive therapy for diversion colitis is surgical reanastomosis.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · THE ATYPICAL COLITIDES | EXTRAINTESTINAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: Which dermatologic manifestation is most commonly associated with Crohn's disease (CD) patients?
items.explanation: Perianal skin tags are found in 75–80% of CD patients, especially those with colon involvement.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · DERMATOLOGIC

items.question: Which dermatologic condition associated with inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) is characterized by hot, red, tender nodules on the lower legs?
items.explanation: Erythema nodosum presents as hot, red, tender nodules on the anterior surface of the lower legs.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · DERMATOLOGIC

items.question: Which dermatologic manifestation can occur years before or after the onset of bowel symptoms in IBD patients?
items.explanation: Pyoderma gangrenosum may occur years before or after the onset of bowel symptoms in IBD patients.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · DERMATOLOGIC

items.question: Which rheumatologic manifestation is most commonly associated with inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) and affects large joints asymmetrically?
items.explanation: Peripheral arthritis is the most common rheumatologic manifestation in IBD, affecting large joints asymmetrically.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · RHEUMATOLOGIC | OCULAR | HEPATOBILIARY

items.question: Which ocular complication of IBD can occur during periods of remission and may develop after bowel resection?
items.explanation: Uveitis is associated with both UC and Crohn's colitis, may be found during periods of remission, and can develop after bowel resection.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · RHEUMATOLOGIC | OCULAR | HEPATOBILIARY

items.question: Which hepatic condition in IBD patients is characterized by inflammation and fibrosis of both intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts?
items.explanation: Primary sclerosing cholangitis is characterized by inflammation and fibrosis of both intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · RHEUMATOLOGIC | OCULAR | HEPATOBILIARY

items.question: Which diagnostic test for primary sclerosing cholangitis is considered safer and can visualize all levels of the biliary tree?
items.explanation: MRCP is sensitive, specific, and safer than ERCP for visualizing all levels of the biliary tree in primary sclerosing cholangitis.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · RHEUMATOLOGIC | OCULAR | HEPATOBILIARY

items.question: Which factor contributes to the development of calcium oxalate stones in patients with Crohn's disease following small-bowel resection?
options: Decreased production of nonabsorbed fatty acids
Increased absorption of dietary oxalate due to ileal dysfunction
items.explanation: In patients with ileal dysfunction, unbound oxalate is absorbed in the colon, leading to hyperoxaluria and stone formation.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · UROLOGIC | METABOLIC BONE DISORDERS

items.question: What is a significant risk factor for low bone mass in patients with inflammatory bowel disease (IBD)?
items.explanation: Chronic glucocorticoid use can lead to significant bone mass loss, increasing the risk for low bone density.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · UROLOGIC | METABOLIC BONE DISORDERS

items.question: Which imaging modality is used in diagnosing osteonecrosis?
items.explanation: Osteonecrosis is diagnosed using bone scans or magnetic resonance imaging (MRI).
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · UROLOGIC | METABOLIC BONE DISORDERS

items.question: Which factor is NOT responsible for the hypercoagulable state in patients with IBD?
items.explanation: Impaired, not increased, fibrinolysis is a factor in IBD-associated hypercoagulability.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · THROMBOEMBOLIC DISORDERS | OTHER DISORDERS | 5-ASA AGENTS

items.question: Which of the following is a rare extraintestinal manifestation (EIM) of IBD that can result from duodenal fistulas?
items.explanation: Pancreatitis is a rare EIM of IBD and can result from duodenal fistulas.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · THROMBOEMBOLIC DISORDERS | OTHER DISORDERS | 5-ASA AGENTS

items.question: Which 5-ASA agent uses Multi-Matrix System (MMX) technology to release mesalamine in the colon?
items.explanation: Lialda uses MMX technology for controlled release of mesalamine in the colon.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · THROMBOEMBOLIC DISORDERS | OTHER DISORDERS | 5-ASA AGENTS

items.question: What is a common side effect shared by all 5-ASA medications?
items.explanation: Headaches are a common side effect across all 5-ASA medications.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · THROMBOEMBOLIC DISORDERS | OTHER DISORDERS | 5-ASA AGENTS

items.question: Which glucocorticoid is specifically designed to be released entirely in the colon and has minimal side effects?
items.explanation: Budesonide is released entirely in the colon with minimal to no glucocorticoid side effects.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · GLUCOCORTICOIDS | ANTIBIOTICS

items.question: For which condition are antibiotics such as metronidazole and ciprofloxacin typically used?
items.explanation: Antibiotics like metronidazole and ciprofloxacin are used for treating pouchitis.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · GLUCOCORTICOIDS | ANTIBIOTICS

items.question: What is the recommended tapering rate of glucocorticoids once clinical remission has been induced in IBD?
items.explanation: Glucocorticoids should be tapered at a rate of no more than 5–10 mg/week.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · GLUCOCORTICOIDS | ANTIBIOTICS

items.question: Which metabolite is the active end product of mercaptopurine metabolism that inhibits purine ribonucleotide synthesis?
items.explanation: Thioinosinic acid is the active end product that inhibits purine ribonucleotide synthesis.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · AZATHIOPRINE AND MERCAPTOPURINE

items.question: Which side effect of azathioprine and mercaptopurine therapy typically presents within the first few weeks?
items.explanation: Pancreatitis occurs in 3–4% of patients and typically presents within the first few weeks.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · AZATHIOPRINE AND MERCAPTOPURINE

items.question: What is a potential risk factor for increased toxicity due to azathioprine or mercaptopurine use?
items.explanation: Individuals lacking thiopurine methyltransferase are at increased risk due to accumulation of active metabolites.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · AZATHIOPRINE AND MERCAPTOPURINE

items.question: Which mechanism is primarily responsible for methotrexate's inhibition of DNA synthesis?
options: Blockade of IL-2 production by T helper lymphocytes
Binding to cyclophilin and inhibiting calcineurin
items.explanation: Methotrexate inhibits dihydrofolate reductase, impairing DNA synthesis.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · METHOTREXATE | CYCLOSPORINE

items.question: What is a common side effect of cyclosporine therapy?
items.explanation: Cyclosporine commonly causes gingival hyperplasia.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · METHOTREXATE | CYCLOSPORINE

items.question: What is the recommended therapeutic range for cyclosporine levels in patients?
items.explanation: Cyclosporine levels should be maintained between 150 and 350 ng/mL.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · METHOTREXATE | CYCLOSPORINE

items.question: Which statement about tacrolimus absorption in Crohn's disease patients is correct?
options: Tacrolimus absorption depends on bile and mucosal integrity.
Tacrolimus has poor oral absorption due to small-bowel involvement.
Tacrolimus shows good oral absorption despite proximal small-bowel involvement.
Tacrolimus is not effective in children with refractory IBD.
items.explanation: Tacrolimus has good oral absorption despite proximal small-bowel Crohn's involvement.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · TACROLIMUS | BIOLOGIC THERAPIES

items.question: What is a reason for the decreasing use of tacrolimus in IBD treatment?
options: Biologic and small-molecule therapies are expanding.
Tacrolimus is ineffective in children with refractory IBD.
Tacrolimus absorption is dependent on bile.
items.explanation: The use of tacrolimus is decreasing due to side effects and the expanding choice of biologic and small-molecule therapies.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · TACROLIMUS | BIOLOGIC THERAPIES

items.question: Which patient group with Crohn's disease (CD) is more likely to require biologics?
options: Patients older than 30 years with mild disease.
Patients with perianal or severe rectal disease and/or deep ulcerations in the colon.
Patients without any complications from CD.
Patients who respond well to conventional therapy.
items.explanation: High-risk patients with CD likely to require biologics include those with perianal or severe rectal disease and/or deep ulcerations in the colon.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · TACROLIMUS | BIOLOGIC THERAPIES

items.question: What is a goal of IBD treatment with biologic therapies?
options: To delay treatment until complications arise.
To aim for deep remission including endoscopic and histologic remission.
To avoid using immunomodulators in combination therapy.
To treat only patients who are refractory to all other treatments.
items.explanation: The goal of IBD treatment with biologics is to aim for deep remission, which includes endoscopic and histologic remission.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · TACROLIMUS | BIOLOGIC THERAPIES

items.question: Which medication is commonly added to anti-TNF therapy to help prevent the formation of antibodies?
items.explanation: It is common practice to add an immunomodulator such as azathioprine, MP, or MTX to anti-TNF therapy to help prevent antibody formation.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · Side Effects of Anti-TNF Therapies

items.question: What should be done if a patient has high anti-infliximab antibodies and a low trough level of infliximab?
items.explanation: If a patient has high anti-infliximab antibodies and a low trough level, it is best to switch to another anti-TNF therapy.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · Side Effects of Anti-TNF Therapies

items.question: What is the baseline risk of Non-Hodgkin's Lymphoma (NHL) in CD patients?
items.explanation: The baseline risk of NHL in CD patients is 2 in 10,000.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · Side Effects of Anti-TNF Therapies

items.question: What type of infections are associated with an increased risk due to anti-TNF drugs?
items.explanation: Anti-TNF drugs are associated with an increased risk of opportunistic fungal infections including disseminated histoplasmosis and coccidioidomycosis.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · Side Effects of Anti-TNF Therapies

items.question: Which integrin subunit combination is specifically targeted by vedolizumab to inhibit leukocyte trafficking to the gut mucosa?
items.explanation: Vedolizumab targets α4β7-integrin, which interacts with MAdCAM-1 to inhibit gut-homing T lymphocytes.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · ANTI-INTEGRINS

items.question: Why is natalizumab no longer used for Crohn's disease (CD) treatment?
options: It increases the risk of PML.
It affects T-cell trafficking to the CNS.
items.explanation: Natalizumab is associated with a risk of progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML).
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · ANTI-INTEGRINS

items.question: Which medication provides gut-selective immunosuppression by targeting α4β7-integrin?
items.explanation: Vedolizumab provides gut-selective immunosuppression by targeting α4β7-integrin.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · ANTI-INTEGRINS

items.question: Which JAK inhibitor is known for having higher specificity for JAK1 and JAK3 than for JAK2 and TYK2?
items.explanation: Tofacitinib has higher specificity for JAK1 and JAK3.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · SMALL MOLECULES

items.question: What is a key regulatory pathway targeted by small molecules in the treatment of IBD?
items.explanation: The JAK/STAT pathway is a key regulatory pathway targeted by small molecules in IBD.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · SMALL MOLECULES

items.question: Which of the following adverse events is associated with an increased risk when using JAK inhibitors?
items.explanation: There is an increased risk of serious adverse events including heart attacks with JAK inhibitors.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · SMALL MOLECULES

items.question: Which nutritional therapy is effective for inducing remission in active Crohn's disease (CD)?
items.explanation: Both EEN and bowel rest with TPN are effective for inducing remission in active CD.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · NUTRITIONAL THERAPIES

items.question: Which dietary component is NOT associated with the onset of inflammatory bowel disease (IBD)?
items.explanation: Maximizing fiber intake from fruits and vegetables is recommended to manage IBD.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · NUTRITIONAL THERAPIES

items.question: Which diet has been shown to be effective for maintenance therapy in Crohn's disease (CD)?
items.explanation: It remains unclear whether any specific diet will lead to evidence-based nutrition guidelines for maintenance therapy in CD.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · NUTRITIONAL THERAPIES

items.question: What is the primary reason for performing an IPAA in patients with ulcerative colitis?
options: To completely remove all inflamed rectal mucosa.
To preserve anal sphincter function and maintain continence.
To prevent any future need for surgical intervention.
To eliminate the risk of pouchitis.
items.explanation: The IPAA operation is designed to preserve the anal sphincter and maintain continence by fashioning an ileal pouch as a neorectum.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · SURGICAL THERAPY

items.question: What percentage of patients with ulcerative colitis undergoing IPAA experience pouch failure necessitating conversion to permanent ileostomy?
items.explanation: Pouch failure requiring conversion to a permanent ileostomy occurs in about 5–10% of patients with ulcerative colitis undergoing IPAA.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · SURGICAL THERAPY

items.question: Which complication is most frequently associated with IPAA in patients with ulcerative colitis?
items.explanation: The most frequent complication of IPAA is pouchitis, occurring in ~30–50% of patients with ulcerative colitis.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · SURGICAL THERAPY

items.question: What surgical alternative should be considered for short strictures with intervening normal mucosa in Crohn's disease?
items.explanation: For short strictures with intervening normal mucosa, strictureplasty is recommended to avoid creating a functionally insufficient length of bowel.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · SURGICAL THERAPY

items.question: Which medication should be discontinued at least 3 months before conception due to its teratogenic effects?
items.explanation: MTX is teratogenic and should be discontinued at least 3 months before conception.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · IBD AND PREGNANCY

items.question: Which antibiotic is recommended to avoid during pregnancy due to potential cartilage lesions in immature animals?
items.explanation: Ciprofloxacin causes cartilage lesions in immature animals and should be avoided during pregnancy.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · IBD AND PREGNANCY

items.question: What is the recommended washout period before conception for tofacitinib and upadacitinib?
items.explanation: A washout period of at least 4 weeks is recommended before conception for tofacitinib and upadacitinib.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · IBD AND PREGNANCY

items.question: Which biologic agent crosses the placenta by passive diffusion, resulting in minimal infant serum levels?
items.explanation: Certolizumab crosses the placenta by passive diffusion, resulting in minimal infant serum levels.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · IBD AND PREGNANCY

items.question: What is the recommended surveillance strategy for patients with ulcerative colitis who have more than 8-10 years of extensive colitis?
options: Annual or biennial colonoscopy with multiple biopsies
No surveillance needed if inflammation is controlled
items.explanation: Annual or biennial colonoscopy with multiple biopsies is recommended for patients with >8–10 years of extensive colitis.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · ULCERATIVE COLITIS | CROHN'S DISEASE

items.question: Which endoscopic technique enhances the visualization of dysplasia compared to standard-definition white light endoscopy?
items.explanation: Chromoendoscopy enhances the visualization of dysplasia compared to standard-definition white light endoscopy.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · ULCERATIVE COLITIS | CROHN'S DISEASE

items.question: What is a noted trend in the risk of colorectal cancer over time for patients with chronic ulcerative colitis?
options: The risk has increased significantly due to poor inflammation control.
There has been no change in the risk over time.
A decrease in risk has been observed, potentially due to better inflammation control and surveillance.
The risk is now lower than in the general population.
items.explanation: A decrease in the risk of colorectal cancer has been noted over time potentially due to better control of inflammation and better colonoscopic surveillance.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · ULCERATIVE COLITIS | CROHN'S DISEASE

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF DYSPLASIA AND CANCER | COVID-19 AND IBD
items.question: What is the usual treatment for flat high-grade dysplasia encountered during colonoscopic surveillance in UC patients?
items.explanation: The usual treatment for flat high-grade dysplasia in UC is colectomy.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · MANAGEMENT OF DYSPLASIA AND CANCER | COVID-19 AND IBD

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF DYSPLASIA AND CANCER | COVID-19 AND IBD
items.question: Which of the following medications used to treat IBD are associated with higher rates of serious viral and bacterial infections?
items.explanation: Glucocorticoids, immunomodulators (thiopurines, MTX), biologics, and JAK inhibitors are associated with higher rates of serious infections.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · MANAGEMENT OF DYSPLASIA AND CANCER | COVID-19 AND IBD

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF DYSPLASIA AND CANCER | COVID-19 AND IBD
items.question: Which factor was NOT found to be associated with severe COVID-19 in IBD patients?
items.explanation: Anti-TNF treatment was not associated with severe COVID-19 (adjusted OR 0.9).
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · MANAGEMENT OF DYSPLASIA AND CANCER | COVID-19 AND IBD

items.subtopic: Inflammatory Bowel Disease | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)
items.statement: The peak incidence of Ulcerative Colitis and Crohn's disease is in the seventh to ninth decades of life.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that the peak incidence for UC and CD is in the second to fourth decades.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · Inflammatory Bowel Disease | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)

items.subtopic: Inflammatory Bowel Disease | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)
items.statement: Vitamin D has a protective effect on the risk of Crohn's disease.
items.explanation: The passage reports that there is a protective effect of vitamin D on the risk of CD.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · Inflammatory Bowel Disease | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)

items.subtopic: GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 2) | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: IBD PHENOTYPES | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.statement: Inflammatory bowel disease is observed exclusively in patients with a family history.
items.explanation: IBD can also be observed in the absence of a family history, known as sporadic disease.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 2) | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: IBD PHENOTYPES | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 2) | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: IBD PHENOTYPES | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.statement: The consensus hypothesis suggests that environmental and genetic factors disrupt homeostasis in the intestines, leading to IBD.
items.explanation: Environmental and genetic factors can cumulatively and interactively disrupt intestinal homeostasis, culminating in chronic inflammation characteristic of IBD.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 2) | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: IBD PHENOTYPES | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.statement: Most of the monogenic mutations related to IBD have been discovered through whole-exome sequencing (WES).
items.explanation: The passage states that most of the monogenic mutations in VEOIBD and infantile IBD have been discovered by WES.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS

items.statement: All genetic loci identified for IBD are specific to either Crohn's disease (CD) or ulcerative colitis (UC).
items.explanation: The passage notes that two-thirds of the genetic loci are associated with both CD and UC, not all are specific to one.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS

items.statement: Humans are born with a fully developed commensal microbiota.
items.explanation: The passage states that humans are born with sterile guts and acquire their microbiota after birth.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · COMMENSAL MICROBIOTA AND IBD

items.statement: Dysbiosis in patients with UC and CD involves an increase in Firmicutes such as Faecalibacterium prausnitzii.
items.explanation: The passage indicates a loss of protective microorganisms like Firmicutes, not an increase.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · COMMENSAL MICROBIOTA AND IBD

items.statement: Oral tolerance is responsible for immune responsiveness to dietary antigens.
items.explanation: The passage states that oral tolerance is responsible for the lack of immune responsiveness to dietary antigens.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · DEFECTIVE IMMUNE REGULATION IN IBD

items.statement: Gene knockout or transgenic mouse models have shown that deleting specific cytokines can lead to spontaneous colitis.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that deleting specific cytokines in these mouse models leads to spontaneous colitis.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · DEFECTIVE IMMUNE REGULATION IN IBD

items.statement: IL-10 and TGF-β are cytokines that promote inflammation in IBD.
items.explanation: IL-10 and TGF-β are anti-inflammatory cytokines that normally limit tissue damage by turning off or inhibiting proinflammatory cytokine activity.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · THE INFLAMMATORY CASCADE IN IBD

items.statement: T_H17 cells may have protective functions in addition to promoting inflammation.
items.explanation: T_H17 cells are noted for their role in neutrophilic recruitment but also possess potential protective functions.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · THE INFLAMMATORY CASCADE IN IBD

items.statement: In ulcerative colitis, the crypts may show a gap between their bases and the muscularis mucosae.
items.explanation: A gap between the crypt bases and the muscularis mucosae is indicative of distorted crypt architecture in UC.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · PATHOLOGY | ULCERATIVE COLITIS: MICROSCOPIC FEATURES

items.statement: In ulcerative colitis, the entire bowel wall is involved histologically.
items.explanation: The inflammatory process in UC is limited to the mucosa and superficial submucosa, except in fulminant disease.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · PATHOLOGY | ULCERATIVE COLITIS: MICROSCOPIC FEATURES

items.subtopic: CROHN'S DISEASE: MACROSCOPIC FEATURES | CROHN'S DISEASE: MICROSCOPIC FEATURES
items.statement: Crohn's disease often involves the rectum.
items.explanation: Unlike ulcerative colitis, the rectum is often spared in Crohn's disease.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE: MACROSCOPIC FEATURES | CROHN'S DISEASE: MICROSCOPIC FEATURES

items.subtopic: CROHN'S DISEASE: MACROSCOPIC FEATURES | CROHN'S DISEASE: MICROSCOPIC FEATURES
items.statement: Transmural inflammation and granulomas are features of Crohn's disease.
items.explanation: Crohn's disease is a transmural process with characteristic noncaseating granulomas.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE: MACROSCOPIC FEATURES | CROHN'S DISEASE: MICROSCOPIC FEATURES

items.statement: Toxic megacolon in ulcerative colitis is defined as a transverse or right colon with a diameter of >6 cm.
items.explanation: Toxic megacolon is defined by these criteria, including the loss of haustration.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · ULCERATIVE COLITIS

items.statement: Perforation in ulcerative colitis is common and easily detected due to obvious physical signs.
items.explanation: Perforation is rare, and the physical signs of peritonitis may not be obvious, especially if patients are receiving glucocorticoids.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · ULCERATIVE COLITIS

items.statement: Enterovesical fistulas in Crohn's disease typically present with pneumaturia or fecaluria.
items.explanation: Enterovesical fistulas can present as dysuria, recurrent bladder infections, pneumaturia, or fecaluria.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE (part 1)

items.statement: Gross bleeding is common in patients with Crohn's colitis.
items.explanation: Gross bleeding is not as common in Crohn's colitis and appears in about one-half of patients with exclusively colonic disease.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE (part 1)

items.statement: Capsule retention occurs in more than 6% of patients with established Crohn's disease.
items.explanation: Retention rates of 4–6% are seen in patients with established CD, not more than 6%.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE (part 2)

items.statement: CT enterography and MR enterography have different accuracies for identifying active small-bowel inflammation.
items.explanation: CT enterography and MR enterography are equally accurate in the identification of active small-bowel inflammation.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: CROHN'S DISEASE (part 3) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS OF UC AND CD | INFECTIOUS DISEASES
items.statement: The peritonitis of free perforation in Crohn's disease may be fatal.
items.explanation: Peritonitis from free perforation, especially colonic, can indeed be fatal.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE (part 3) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS OF UC AND CD | INFECTIOUS DISEASES

items.subtopic: CROHN'S DISEASE (part 3) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS OF UC AND CD | INFECTIOUS DISEASES
items.statement: Serologic markers are more helpful than clinical factors at diagnosis in predicting the natural history of IBD.
items.explanation: Clinical factors described at diagnosis are more helpful than serologies for predicting the natural history of IBD.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE (part 3) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS OF UC AND CD | INFECTIOUS DISEASES

items.statement: Ischemic bowel disease should be considered in patients with a history of abdominal aortic aneurysm repair.
items.explanation: Ischemic colitis is more likely to occur in the elderly following abdominal aortic aneurysm repair.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · NONINFECTIOUS DISEASES

items.statement: Biopsies are not useful in diagnosing solitary rectal ulcer syndrome.
items.explanation: Biopsies can be diagnostic for solitary rectal ulcer syndrome.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · NONINFECTIOUS DISEASES

items.statement: Diversion colitis can be differentiated from ulcerative colitis based on histopathology.
items.explanation: Diversion colitis shows normal crypt architecture, differentiating it from UC.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · THE ATYPICAL COLITIDES | EXTRAINTESTINAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.statement: Celiac disease should be excluded in patients with collagenous colitis.
items.explanation: Celiac disease exclusion is specifically recommended for lymphocytic colitis.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · THE ATYPICAL COLITIDES | EXTRAINTESTINAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.statement: Erythema nodosum lesions usually develop before the onset of bowel symptoms.
items.explanation: Erythema nodosum lesions typically develop after the onset of bowel symptoms.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · DERMATOLOGIC

items.statement: Psoriasis in IBD patients is related to bowel activity.
items.explanation: Psoriasis affects 5–10% of IBD patients and is unrelated to bowel activity.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · DERMATOLOGIC

items.statement: Peripheral arthritis in IBD patients is symmetric and affects small joints.
items.explanation: Peripheral arthritis in IBD is asymmetric, polyarticular, migratory, and most often affects large joints.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · RHEUMATOLOGIC | OCULAR | HEPATOBILIARY

items.statement: Patients with primary sclerosing cholangitis have a 10-15% lifetime risk of developing cholangiocarcinoma.
items.explanation: PSC patients indeed have a 10-15% lifetime risk of developing cholangiocarcinoma.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · RHEUMATOLOGIC | OCULAR | HEPATOBILIARY

items.statement: The absolute risk of an osteoporotic fracture in IBD patients is approximately 5% per person per year.
items.explanation: The absolute risk of an osteoporotic fracture is ~1% per person per year.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · UROLOGIC | METABOLIC BONE DISORDERS

items.statement: Osteonecrosis more frequently affects the knees than the hips.
items.explanation: Osteonecrosis affects the hips more often than the knees.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · UROLOGIC | METABOLIC BONE DISORDERS

items.statement: Sulfasalazine is effective for treating mild to moderate UC but has a high rate of side effects.
items.explanation: Sulfasalazine is indeed used for mild to moderate UC, but its use can be limited by significant side effects.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · THROMBOEMBOLIC DISORDERS | OTHER DISORDERS | 5-ASA AGENTS

items.statement: Renal function tests and urinalysis should be checked monthly for patients on 5-ASA medications.
items.explanation: The passage recommends checking renal function tests and urinalysis yearly, not monthly.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · THROMBOEMBOLIC DISORDERS | OTHER DISORDERS | 5-ASA AGENTS

items.statement: Topical 5-ASA therapy is less effective than topical steroid therapy in the treatment of distal UC.
items.explanation: Topical 5-ASA therapy is more effective than topical steroid therapy for distal UC.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · GLUCOCORTICOIDS | ANTIBIOTICS

items.statement: Antibiotics play a role in the treatment of active ulcerative colitis.
items.explanation: Antibiotics have no role in the treatment of active or quiescent UC.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · GLUCOCORTICOIDS | ANTIBIOTICS

items.statement: Bone marrow suppression from azathioprine and mercaptopurine is immediate.
items.explanation: Bone marrow suppression is dose-related and often delayed.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · AZATHIOPRINE AND MERCAPTOPURINE

items.statement: Thiopurine-associated lymphoma risk increases with age in IBD patients on azathioprine.
items.explanation: The highest risk is in patients >65 years old, with a moderate risk for those 50-65.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · AZATHIOPRINE AND MERCAPTOPURINE

items.statement: Hypersensitivity pneumonitis is a common complication of methotrexate therapy.
items.explanation: Hypersensitivity pneumonitis is a rare but serious complication of methotrexate.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · METHOTREXATE | CYCLOSPORINE

items.statement: Creatinine elevation in cyclosporine therapy may necessitate dose reduction or discontinuation.
items.explanation: Creatinine elevation calls for dose adjustment or stopping cyclosporine.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · METHOTREXATE | CYCLOSPORINE

items.statement: Infliximab was the first biologic therapy approved for moderately to severely active inflammatory and fistulizing CD and UC.
items.explanation: Infliximab, a chimeric IgG1 antibody against TNF-α, was indeed the first biologic therapy approved for these conditions.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · TACROLIMUS | BIOLOGIC THERAPIES

items.statement: Adalimumab has higher immunogenicity compared to infliximab.
items.explanation: Adalimumab is noted for having less immunogenicity than infliximab.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · TACROLIMUS | BIOLOGIC THERAPIES

items.statement: Anti-TNF medications are associated with an increased risk of lymphoma after adjustment for co-treatments.
items.explanation: After adjustment for co-treatments, no excess risk of lymphoma was found in a Danish study of IBD patients exposed to anti-TNF medications.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · Side Effects of Anti-TNF Therapies

items.statement: Patients on anti-TNF therapy should have an annual skin check due to increased risks.
items.explanation: The risk of nonmelanoma skin cancer is increased with thiopurines and biologics, so patients should have a skin check at least once a year.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · Side Effects of Anti-TNF Therapies

items.statement: Natalizumab inhibits T-cell trafficking to the central nervous system.
items.explanation: Natalizumab inhibits T-cell trafficking to the CNS, contributing to its risk of PML.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · ANTI-INTEGRINS

items.statement: Ustekinumab targets IL-12 and IL-23 by binding their p19 subunit.
items.explanation: Ustekinumab blocks the common p40 subunit of IL-12 and IL-23, not the p19 subunit.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · ANTI-INTEGRINS

items.statement: Ozanimod binds selectively to S1P receptor subtypes S1P1 and S1P5.
items.explanation: Ozanimod is a selective modulator for the S1P1 and S1P5 receptor subtypes.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · SMALL MOLECULES

items.statement: Tofacitinib is approved as a first-line treatment for IBD.
items.explanation: Tofacitinib is FDA approved for use as a second-line agent after failure of anti-TNF therapy.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · SMALL MOLECULES

items.statement: Active ulcerative colitis (UC) responds effectively to elemental diets.
items.explanation: Elemental diets or TPN are not effective treatments for active UC.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · NUTRITIONAL THERAPIES

items.statement: Dietary components may interact with the gut microbiome and stimulate a mucosal immune response.
items.explanation: Diet influences the gut microbiome and can affect mucosal immunity.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · NUTRITIONAL THERAPIES

items.statement: Patients with Crohn's disease who undergo surgery are guaranteed not to have any recurrence of the disease.
items.explanation: Recurrence is common in Crohn's disease, and risk factors include cigarette smoking and penetrating disease.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · SURGICAL THERAPY

items.statement: An IPAA is contraindicated in patients with Crohn's colitis due to a high incidence of pouch failure.
items.explanation: Most surgeons believe an IPAA is contraindicated in CD because of the high rate of pouch failure.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · SURGICAL THERAPY

items.statement: Sulfasalazine and mesalamines are safe for use during pregnancy without any additional supplementation.
items.explanation: Additional folate supplementation must be given with sulfasalazine.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · IBD AND PREGNANCY

items.statement: Patients with quiescent UC and CD have normal fertility rates.
items.explanation: Quiescent UC and CD do not affect fertility rates adversely.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · IBD AND PREGNANCY

items.statement: The rates of colon cancer in patients with ulcerative colitis are about the same as in the general population.
items.explanation: The rates of colon cancer are still about 1.5–2 times higher than in the general population.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · ULCERATIVE COLITIS | CROHN'S DISEASE

items.statement: A pediatric colonoscope can be used to pass narrow strictures in Crohn's disease patients.
items.explanation: A pediatric colonoscope can indeed be used to pass narrow strictures in CD patients.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · ULCERATIVE COLITIS | CROHN'S DISEASE

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF DYSPLASIA AND CANCER | COVID-19 AND IBD
items.statement: Patients with CD have an increased risk of NHL, leukemia, and myelodysplastic syndromes.
items.explanation: CD patients are at greater risk for these malignancies.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · MANAGEMENT OF DYSPLASIA AND CANCER | COVID-19 AND IBD

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF DYSPLASIA AND CANCER | COVID-19 AND IBD
items.statement: Anti-TNF treatment is associated with severe COVID-19 in IBD patients.
items.explanation: Anti-TNF treatment was not found to be associated with severe COVID-19.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · MANAGEMENT OF DYSPLASIA AND CANCER | COVID-19 AND IBD

items.subtopic: Inflammatory Bowel Disease | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)
items.assertion: The incidence of Inflammatory Bowel Disease is rising in newly industrialized countries.
items.reason: This increase mirrors the prior rise of IBD in Western countries during the twentieth century due to urbanization and Westernization.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · Inflammatory Bowel Disease | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)

items.subtopic: Inflammatory Bowel Disease | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)
items.assertion: Breast-feeding may protect against the development of IBD.
items.reason: This supports the potential importance of early-life exposures to later IBD development.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · Inflammatory Bowel Disease | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)

items.subtopic: GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 2) | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: IBD PHENOTYPES | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.assertion: IBD is considered an inappropriate immune response to the commensal microbiota.
items.reason: A search for infectious agents in IBD has been unrewarding, indicating a misdirected immune response rather than infection.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; the lack of identified infectious agents supports the view that IBD is an inappropriate immune response.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 2) | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: IBD PHENOTYPES | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 2) | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: IBD PHENOTYPES | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.assertion: Older Asian patients with UC tend to have a less aggressive disease course.
items.reason: Age-related differences in the aggressiveness of UC are observed among different ethnic groups.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; older Asian patients with UC actually tend to have a more aggressive disease course.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 2) | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS: IBD PHENOTYPES | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.assertion: IBD has a familial origin in at least 10% of afflicted adults.
items.reason: This is consistent with an inherited basis for the disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS

items.assertion: The genetic risk factors identified for IBD are only associated with Crohn's disease (CD) and not other immune-mediated diseases.
items.reason: This is because these genetic risk factors are specific to the pathogenesis of CD.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as the passage states that many genetic risk factors for IBD are also associated with other immune-mediated diseases.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS

items.assertion: Inflammatory cytokines such as IL-1, IL-6, and TNF are involved in promoting inflammation in IBD.
items.reason: These cytokines promote fibrogenesis, collagen production, and activation of tissue metalloproteinases.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains how these cytokines contribute to inflammation.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · THE INFLAMMATORY CASCADE IN IBD

items.assertion: Anti-IL-12 therapy is ineffective in treating IBD.
items.reason: IL-12 initiates the T_H1 pathway, which is involved in mucosal inflammation.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because anti-IL-12 therapy can be effective; IL-12's role in initiating T_H1 pathways makes it a valid therapeutic target.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · THE INFLAMMATORY CASCADE IN IBD

items.subtopic: CROHN'S DISEASE: MACROSCOPIC FEATURES | CROHN'S DISEASE: MICROSCOPIC FEATURES
items.assertion: Crohn's disease is characterized by a 'cobblestone' appearance.
items.reason: This appearance results from stellate ulcerations fusing longitudinally and transversely to form islands of mucosa.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the cobblestone appearance in Crohn's disease.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE: MACROSCOPIC FEATURES | CROHN'S DISEASE: MICROSCOPIC FEATURES

items.assertion: Strictures in ulcerative colitis should always be presumed malignant until proven otherwise.
items.reason: Strictures that prevent passage of the colonoscope are an indication for surgery due to potential malignancy.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why strictures must be presumed malignant.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · ULCERATIVE COLITIS

items.assertion: Fecal calprotectin levels do not correlate with histologic inflammation.
items.reason: Fecal calprotectin is present in neutrophils and monocytes, and its levels correlate well with histologic inflammation.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because fecal calprotectin levels do indeed correlate with histologic inflammation.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · ULCERATIVE COLITIS

items.assertion: Diarrhea in active Crohn's disease can be caused by bacterial overgrowth.
items.reason: Bacterial overgrowth occurs due to obstructive stasis or fistulization.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE (part 1)

items.assertion: Rectovaginal fistulas in Crohn's disease develop without any prior surgical intervention.
items.reason: Rectovaginal fistulas are rare and often follow tissue planes of least resistance.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because rectovaginal fistulas are unlikely to develop without a prior hysterectomy.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE (part 1)

items.assertion: Wireless capsule endoscopy is not recommended for patients with a known small-bowel stricture.
items.reason: Capsule retention occurs in <1% of patients with suspected CD, but higher rates are seen in established CD.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why WCE is not recommended for known strictures.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE (part 2)

items.assertion: Pelvic MRI is superior to pelvic CT in demonstrating pelvic lesions.
items.reason: MRI offers better soft tissue contrast and avoids radiation exposure.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not directly explain why MRI is superior for pelvic lesions.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: CROHN'S DISEASE (part 3) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS OF UC AND CD | INFECTIOUS DISEASES
items.assertion: CMV colitis can occur in immunocompetent individuals.
items.reason: CMV occurs most commonly in the esophagus, colon, and rectum but may also involve the small intestine.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains that CMV can occur beyond immunocompromised patients.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE (part 3) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS OF UC AND CD | INFECTIOUS DISEASES

items.subtopic: CROHN'S DISEASE (part 3) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS OF UC AND CD | INFECTIOUS DISEASES
items.assertion: Yersinia enterocolitica infection causes symptoms primarily in the colon.
items.reason: Yersinia enterocolitica infection occurs mainly in the terminal ileum and causes mucosal ulceration, neutrophil invasion, and thickening of the ileal wall.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because Yersinia enterocolitica primarily affects the terminal ileum, not the colon.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · CROHN'S DISEASE (part 3) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS OF UC AND CD | INFECTIOUS DISEASES

items.assertion: Endoscopic recurrence following segmental resection favors a diagnosis of Crohn's disease.
items.reason: Crohn's disease is characterized by transmural inflammation and can recur after surgery.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why endoscopic recurrence following segmental resection favors CD.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · NONINFECTIOUS DISEASES

items.assertion: NSAID-related colitis can lead to severe complications such as perforation.
items.reason: NSAIDs cause inflammation of the colon by inhibiting prostaglandin synthesis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why NSAID-related colitis can lead to severe complications.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · NONINFECTIOUS DISEASES

items.assertion: Ankylosing spondylitis activity in IBD patients is related to bowel disease activity.
items.reason: AS does not correlate with bowel activity and does not remit with glucocorticoids or colectomy.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because AS activity is independent of bowel disease activity.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · RHEUMATOLOGIC | OCULAR | HEPATOBILIARY

items.assertion: Gallbladder polyps in patients with primary sclerosing cholangitis have a low incidence of malignancy.
items.reason: Cholecystectomy is recommended for gallbladder polyps even if they are <1 cm due to high malignancy risk.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because gallbladder polyps in PSC have a high incidence of malignancy.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · RHEUMATOLOGIC | OCULAR | HEPATOBILIARY

items.assertion: Sulfasalazine can impair folate absorption.
items.reason: Patients on sulfasalazine should be given folic acid supplements.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the supplementation is due to impaired folate absorption by sulfasalazine.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · THROMBOEMBOLIC DISORDERS | OTHER DISORDERS | 5-ASA AGENTS

items.assertion: Pentasa uses an ethylcellulose coating for controlled release.
items.reason: The microspheres disperse throughout the entire GI tract from small intestine to distal colon.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the dispersion is facilitated by the ethylcellulose coating.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · THROMBOEMBOLIC DISORDERS | OTHER DISORDERS | 5-ASA AGENTS

items.assertion: Budesonide is used for maintenance therapy in both ulcerative colitis and Crohn's disease.
items.reason: Glucocorticoids play no role in maintenance therapy in either UC or CD.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; glucocorticoids do not serve as maintenance therapy for UC or CD.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · GLUCOCORTICOIDS | ANTIBIOTICS

items.assertion: Methotrexate is often used with biologic therapy to improve disease response.
items.reason: It decreases antibody formation against the biologic agent.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · METHOTREXATE | CYCLOSPORINE

items.assertion: Combination therapy with infliximab and azathioprine is more effective for moderate to severe CD than either agent alone.
items.reason: Randomized trials support combination therapy over monotherapy for these conditions.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · TACROLIMUS | BIOLOGIC THERAPIES

items.assertion: Certolizumab pegol is administered orally for CD.
items.reason: Certolizumab pegol is a pegylated form of an anti-TNF Fab portion of an antibody.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because certolizumab pegol is administered subcutaneously, not orally.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · TACROLIMUS | BIOLOGIC THERAPIES

items.assertion: Patients on anti-TNF therapy are recommended to undergo PPD or QuantiFERON-TB Gold testing.
items.reason: This is because all of the anti-TNF drugs are associated with an increased risk of reactivation of latent tuberculosis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why patients should be tested before starting anti-TNF therapy.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · Side Effects of Anti-TNF Therapies

items.assertion: Patients with high anti-infliximab antibodies should continue infliximab treatment.
items.reason: High anti-infliximab antibodies are associated with a decreased response to treatment.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because it is best to switch to another anti-TNF therapy if high anti-infliximab antibodies and low trough levels are present.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · Side Effects of Anti-TNF Therapies

items.assertion: Small molecules can diffuse through cell membranes to inhibit cytokine signaling pathways.
items.reason: This mechanism may be more efficacious because several cytokine pathways are involved in IBD pathogenesis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why small molecules might be effective.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · SMALL MOLECULES

items.assertion: The majority of patients with Crohn's disease will require at least one operation during their lifetime.
items.reason: Surgery is an option only when medical treatment has failed or complications dictate its necessity.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why most patients with Crohn's disease will require surgery.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · SURGICAL THERAPY

items.assertion: Pouchitis in patients with IPAA usually responds to antibiotics.
items.reason: Pouch biopsies can distinguish true pouchitis from underlying Crohn's disease.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why pouchitis usually responds to antibiotics.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · SURGICAL THERAPY

items.assertion: Women with an IPAA have decreased fertility.
items.reason: This is due to scarring or occlusion of the fallopian tubes secondary to pelvic inflammation and adhesions.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.

items.assertion: MTX can be used safely during pregnancy if discontinued at least 3 months before conception.
items.reason: MTX is teratogenic and should not be used during pregnancy.
items.explanation: A is true, but R is false; MTX cannot be used safely during pregnancy.
items.reference: Inflammatory Bowel Disease · IBD AND PREGNANCY
